theory_description: Agents that play text games using language models are best served by a hierarchical memory architecture that separates (1) detailed episodic traces (raw transcripts, recent observations, exact action sequences), (2) compressed schemas/semantic summaries (location types, object affordances, quest templates), and (3) prospective (intention/subgoal) memory holding short conditional plans. The hierarchy trades off recoverable detail for generalization and supports efficient retrieval, long-horizon credit assignment, and reuse of strategies under partial observability and limited retrieval bandwidth.

law.theory_statement: For LM-based agents in text games with constrained memory-retrieval bandwidth, organizing memory into at least two levels — detailed episodic traces E and compressed schemas S — yields higher task performance P for a fixed total token/storage budget than a flat memory representation. Performance is approximately a monotonic function of an effective-memory utility M_eff = α|S| + β|E| where α > β > 0 (α and β are per-unit utility weights reflecting schema generality vs episodic specificity). When tasks demand long-horizon reasoning and recurring structures, marginal utility per unit of S exceeds that of E.
law.domain_scope: Applies to agents whose controller is an autoregressive language model (transformer-family or similar) that (a) generates actions as text tokens, (b) experiences partially observable, language-described states (interactive fiction / text-adventure / TextWorld / Jericho-style environments), (c) operates under bounded per-step context/token budgets (limited retrieval/conditioning tokens), and (d) faces tasks requiring multi-step strategies across tens-to-hundreds of steps with recurring substructures.
special_cases: Environments with effectively no recurrence or shared structure (high-entropy stochastic descriptions) reduce the value of S; α ≈ β.
Extremely short episodes (≤ ~5 steps) or single-shot tasks where no generalization is useful make hierarchical indexing overhead detrimental.
When memory indexing/retrieval is near-perfect (very high precision), raw episodic capacity E can approach the utility of compressed schemas.

supporting_evidence.text: Memory-augmented neural architectures (Memory Networks, DNC) separate stored traces and retrieved content, improving QA and sequential tasks.
Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) and nearest-neighbor LMs show large early gains from a small, well-indexed retrieval set rather than a large unindexed cache.
Text game benchmarks (TextWorld, Jericho) and analyses show recurring room types, object affordances, and repeatable quest/recipe patterns that are amenable to schema abstraction.
Hierarchical RL (options, skills) demonstrates that abstractions enabling reuse improve long-horizon performance and sample efficiency.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Episodic vs semantic memory distinctions, retrieval-augmented LMs, and hierarchical RL are established; prior work shows retrieval and abstraction both help sequential tasks.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: HEPMT explicitly prescribes an LM-centered hierarchical memory with a quantitative effective-utility decomposition M_eff = α|S| + β|E| and the operational claim α > β for long-horizon text-game tasks under bounded retrieval budgets.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: This statement synthesizes known components into a specific utility-based decomposition for LM agents in text games; the pieces exist but the combined, prescriptive law and domain focus are novel.
references: Sukhbaatar et al. (2015) End-to-End Memory Networks [episodic-style retrieval for language tasks]
Graves et al. (2016) Differentiable Neural Computer [content-addressable memory architectures]
Lewis et al. (2020) Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) [retrieval improves LM outputs]
Côté et al. (2018) TextWorld: A Learning Environment for Text-based Games [benchmark and recurring structure]

theory_description: Agents that play text games using language models are best served by a hierarchical memory architecture that separates (1) detailed episodic traces (raw transcripts, recent observations, exact action sequences), (2) compressed schemas/semantic summaries (location types, object affordances, quest templates), and (3) prospective (intention/subgoal) memory holding short conditional plans. The hierarchy trades off recoverable detail for generalization and supports efficient retrieval, long-horizon credit assignment, and reuse of strategies under partial observability and limited retrieval bandwidth.

law.theory_statement: Explicit storage of short prospective plans (conditional subgoal sequences of length L ≈ 2–8, with attached verification hooks) reduces expected re-planning cost CR roughly proportional to the fraction φ of repeated subgoals successfully retained and reused: CR ∝ (1 - φ). Storing prospective plans improves average-step efficiency and reduces repeated exploratory backtracking when plans are reliably verified upon retrieval.
law.domain_scope: Applies when agents encounter recurring multi-step subroutines (e.g., fetch-assemble-use workflows, door-unlock sequences) in text games, and when plan execution can be interrupted or requires recovery after exploration; assumes plans are stored with contextual triggers (state fingerprints) enabling targeted retrieval.
special_cases: If plan outcomes are highly non-deterministic or environment dynamics change between attempts, retained plans may mislead and increase wasted actions.
Adversarial paraphrasing or deliberate state obfuscation without robust state fingerprints reduces utility of stored plans.
Very long prospective plans (L≫8) become brittle and costly to maintain; effectiveness concentrates on short chunks.

supporting_evidence.text: Hierarchical RL/options literature shows reusable subpolicies reduce planning overhead; episodic control caches successful trajectories for faster reuse.
Human prospective memory research demonstrates that storing intentions reduces planning load and improves task completion rates in interrupted tasks.
Practical text-game agents that cache short successful action sequences or recipes empirically show efficiency gains on recurring quests.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Options and subpolicy reuse are standard in RL; simple action-sequence caching has been used in text-game agents.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: Formalizing prospective memory as a first-class memory partition for LM agents with a compact predictive relationship for re-planning cost (CR ∝ (1 - φ)) and prescribing verification hooks and conditional triggers tied to language fingerprints.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: This extends existing hierarchical/subgoal reuse ideas into a memory engineering principle for LM-controller agents interacting through text.
references: Sutton et al. (1999) Between MDPs and Semi-MDPs: Options [subgoal reuse]
Blundell et al. (2016) Model-Free Episodic Control [trajectory caching]
Bower & Morrow (1990) Prospective memory studies [human evidence for intention storage]

theory_description: Agents that play text games using language models are best served by a hierarchical memory architecture that separates (1) detailed episodic traces (raw transcripts, recent observations, exact action sequences), (2) compressed schemas/semantic summaries (location types, object affordances, quest templates), and (3) prospective (intention/subgoal) memory holding short conditional plans. The hierarchy trades off recoverable detail for generalization and supports efficient retrieval, long-horizon credit assignment, and reuse of strategies under partial observability and limited retrieval bandwidth.

law.theory_statement: When per-step retrieval tokens/time are constrained and retrieval precision does not improve with storage, marginal performance gain ΔP from increasing raw episodic memory |E| follows diminishing returns approximated by ΔP/Δ|E| ∝ 1/|E| (i.e., P ∝ log |E|) beyond an initial regime; allocating added capacity to compressed schemas S yields larger early returns when environment structure repeats.
law.domain_scope: Applies to transformer-based LM agents with (a) hard per-decision context token limits and (b) retrieval systems whose precision (hit-rate for relevant entries) is approximately constant as |E| grows; environments must contain repeated structure so that compressed schemas capture recurring information.
special_cases: If indexing/retrieval precision improves with capacity (learned keys, hierarchical indices), returns can exceed logarithmic behavior.
If episodic entries are highly redundant, effective |E| is smaller and the law overestimates marginal benefits.
If retrieval budget is effectively unlimited, diminishing-returns constraint is relaxed.

supporting_evidence.text: Empirical RAG and nearest-neighbor cache studies show large early gains from small, well-chosen retrieval sets with tapering improvements as raw cache grows without better indexing.
General scaling/diminishing-return patterns for models and datasets (Kaplan et al.) and intuition from information theory about marginal information in random additional transcripts.
Practical constraints in deployed LMs: token windows, latency budgets, and cost-per-token which limit effective retrieval per step.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Scaling laws and caching performance curves are well-documented; retrieval literature notes tapering gains.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: Applying a specific logarithmic-style diminishing-returns guideline to episodic memory for LM agents in bounded-retrieval text-game settings and deriving an actionable recommendation to prioritize compressed schema capacity.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: This is an application/extension of existing scaling concepts into memory-augmentation engineering for LM-driven interactive agents.
references: Kaplan et al. (2020) Scaling Laws for Neural Language Models [diminishing returns in model/data scaling]
Khandelwal et al. (2020) Nearest Neighbor Language Models [cache/retrieval performance patterns]
Lewis et al. (2020) Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) [benefit from small retrieved contexts]

theory_description: A unified, differentiable control policy for episodic key-value memory in language-model (LM) agents playing text games. UDDMC gates writes based on probabilistic surprise multiplied by an estimator of expected future utility, modulated by an explicit storage cost; it controls retrieval concentration via a learned mapping from epistemic uncertainty to softmax temperature; and it implements end-to-end differentiable reads/writes so reward gradients backpropagate to memory-addressing and content. The result is sparse, high-utility memory contents, focused retrieval when the agent is uncertain, and improved long-horizon credit assignment for textual decision sequences.

law.theory_statement: Write o_t to episodic key-value memory iff Surprise(o_t) * EstimatedExpectedUtilityGain(o_t) > lambda * StorageCost, where Surprise(o_t) = -log p_model(o_t | context); EstimatedExpectedUtilityGain is a learned (or bootstrapped) estimate of the marginal increase in future return enabled by retaining o_t; lambda is a resource-cost scalar. The write operation is implemented as a differentiable gating scalar in [0,1] (soft write) so gradients from downstream rewards can update both content encodings and the utility estimator.
law.domain_scope: Applies to transformer-based LM agents interacting with text-based interactive environments (e.g., TextWorld/Jericho-style games) where (1) observations are token sequences, (2) memory capacity or per-step retrieval token budget is constrained, and (3) the agent is trained with gradient-based methods (supervised fine-tuning and/or policy gradients). Not intended for purely symbolic planners without learnable likelihood models, nor for settings where observation likelihoods are unavailable or meaningless (e.g., adversarial token streams without a trainable model).
special_cases: Purely random environments: Surprise is high but EstimatedExpectedUtilityGain is near-zero; the multiplicative rule prevents wasteful writes.
Zero or negligible storage cost (lambda -> 0): gating collapses toward frequent writes (degenerates to write-every-step policy).
When the utility estimator is poorly trained early in learning, use curriculum (bootstrapped TD targets or auxiliary supervised labels) or conservative thresholds to avoid pathological writes.
Discrete write constraints (hard capacity) can be approximated by soft gates + periodic hard pruning based on learned importance.

supporting_evidence.text: Neuroscience: novelty/surprise signals bias hippocampal encoding and long-term consolidation (novelty-triggered memory formation).
Reinforcement learning: prioritized experience replay stores and samples transitions by priority (e.g., TD-error) to focus learning on high-utility experiences (Schaul et al. 2015).
Episodic control: model-free episodic caches store high-return episodes for rapid retrieval (Blundell et al. 2016).
Likelihood-based novelty measures are commonly available from LMs (next-token probabilities) and have been used to detect surprising inputs.
Complementary learning systems theory: consolidation transfers episodic traces into more stable representations when they are behaviorally significant (McClelland et al. 1995).
Practical LM + memory systems (RAG, RETRO) demonstrate gains from selective retrieval and storage of relevant text chunks, suggesting write selection matters for performance under retrieval budgets.
Temporal-difference/value estimation provides standard mechanisms to estimate marginal utility of stored experiences (Sutton & Barto).

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Surprise/novelty-driven encoding and priority-based storage policies exist in neuroscience and RL (novelty bonuses, prioritized replay, episodic control). LMs provide next-token likelihoods usable as surprise proxies.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: The explicit multiplicative gate combining probabilistic surprise with a learned ExpectedUtilityGain, scaled by an explicit storage-cost lambda, implemented as a differentiable soft-write gate tightly integrated with transformer-based LM agents in text-game settings.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: Synthesis of existing components into a differentiable gate that is directly trainable end-to-end with LM logits and reward gradients—applies known concepts but introduces a concrete, trainable rule and implementation constraints for language agents.
references: Schaul et al. (2015) Prioritized Experience Replay [priority-based replay]
Blundell et al. (2016) Model-Free Episodic Control [episodic caching]
McClelland et al. (1995) Why there are complementary learning systems in the hippocampus and neocortex [consolidation]
Lewis et al. (2020) RAG: Retrieval-Augmented Generation [selective retrieval in LMs]
Borgeaud et al. (2022) RETRO: Cutting Transformer Retrieval [retrieval-augmented LM]

theory_description: A unified, differentiable control policy for episodic key-value memory in language-model (LM) agents playing text games. UDDMC gates writes based on probabilistic surprise multiplied by an estimator of expected future utility, modulated by an explicit storage cost; it controls retrieval concentration via a learned mapping from epistemic uncertainty to softmax temperature; and it implements end-to-end differentiable reads/writes so reward gradients backpropagate to memory-addressing and content. The result is sparse, high-utility memory contents, focused retrieval when the agent is uncertain, and improved long-horizon credit assignment for textual decision sequences.

law.theory_statement: Memory retrieval attention scores use softmax(key·query / tau(u)), where tau(u) is a learned, differentiable temperature function of epistemic uncertainty u about the current state; tau(u) monotonically decreases with u, producing sharper (more concentrated) retrieval distributions when the agent is more uncertain, and broader retrieval (higher tau) when the agent is confident to expose richer context.
law.domain_scope: Applies where (1) memory retrieval is implemented by differentiable attention over key-value stores, (2) per-step token budget limits the number of retrieved tokens that can condition the LM, and (3) an epistemic uncertainty estimate is available (e.g., ensemble variance, MC-dropout variance, logits-entropy calibrated by augmentations). Not applicable when no credible uncertainty estimate can be computed or when retrieval must be hard/one-hot by design.
special_cases: Poor or adversarial uncertainty estimates may cause overconfident or underconfident retrieval; calibration procedures or robust estimators (ensembles, deep ensembles) are recommended.
Very low tau (near-zero) can cause brittle single-memory reliance; add entropy regularization or floor temperature to avoid collapse.
When retrieval budget is large (effectively unlimited), temperature control is less impactful and can be neutralized.

supporting_evidence.text: Softmax temperature controls distribution concentration; used widely in attention and sampling.

Uncertainty-aware attention and selection have precedent in active learning and some memory/attention works; LM uncertainty signals (ensembles, MC-dropout, deep ensembles) are practical and informative for calibration.
Retrieval-augmented LMs (RAG, RETRO) show the importance of selecting high-quality contexts — concentration on few high-value contexts is beneficial when retrieval is costly.
Calibration and ensemble techniques (Lakshminarayanan et al. 2017; Gal & Ghahramani 2016) provide operational uncertainty metrics suitable for tau(u).

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Temperature scaling and uncertainty estimation are established; attention mechanisms and retrieval-augmented LMs use scoring functions.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: A continuous differentiable mapping from LM epistemic uncertainty to retrieval temperature tau, used as an operational knob to trade off concentration vs. breadth under token budgets for text-game agents.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: The components exist separately; coupling uncertainty to retrieval temperature as a learned differentiable module targeted to token-limited gameplay is novel and actionable.
references: Gal & Ghahramani (2016) Dropout as a Bayesian Approximation [MC-dropout uncertainty]
Lakshminarayanan et al. (2017) Deep Ensembles [practical uncertainty]
Lewis et al. (2020) RAG [retrieval selection]
Borgeaud et al. (2022) RETRO [retrieval-augmented LM]

theory_description: A unified, differentiable control policy for episodic key-value memory in language-model (LM) agents playing text games. UDDMC gates writes based on probabilistic surprise multiplied by an estimator of expected future utility, modulated by an explicit storage cost; it controls retrieval concentration via a learned mapping from epistemic uncertainty to softmax temperature; and it implements end-to-end differentiable reads/writes so reward gradients backpropagate to memory-addressing and content. The result is sparse, high-utility memory contents, focused retrieval when the agent is uncertain, and improved long-horizon credit assignment for textual decision sequences.

law.statement_name: Differentiable Credit Assignment Through Memory Replay
law.theory_statement: Making write and read operations soft/differentiable allows gradients from delayed textual rewards to flow back through retrieval attention weights and write gates to the original stored encodings; this lowers variance of long-horizon credit signals and enables the model to learn which observations causally influenced future returns, improving sample-efficiency over purely heuristic, non-differentiable replay scheduling.
law.domain_scope: Applies to agents trained with gradient-based learning (supervised fine-tuning, actor-critic/policy gradients with reparameterization where possible) and using soft attention addressing in memory modules (e.g., Neural Turing Machine, DNC, Memory Networks). Not directly applicable to hard-coded episodic buffers with discrete addresses unless relaxed differentiably.
special_cases: On-policy RL with non-differentiable sampling (e.g., discrete action sampling without reparameterization) still requires variance reduction techniques (baselines/advantage).
Very long horizons can make end-to-end differentiation computationally heavy; truncated backpropagation through time or episodic credit proxies may be necessary.
Where reward sparsity is extreme, gradients through memory alone may be insufficient without additional auxiliary losses (behavior cloning, value prediction).

supporting_evidence.text: Differentiable memory architectures (NTM, DNC, End-to-end Memory Networks) allow gradient flow through read/write addressing and improve tasks requiring long-term dependencies.

Non-differentiable prioritized replay requires heuristics to assign importance and cannot directly receive gradient signals from future returns; prioritized replay still effective but limited for direct credit assignment.

Empirical RL literature shows that structured memory and attention mechanisms can reduce variance and improve learning on long-horizon tasks when integrated with gradients (Graves et al. 2016; Kaiser et al. works).

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Differentiable memory architectures and prioritized replay are established; applying gradient-based credit assignment through attention/read-write mechanisms is known in sequence modeling.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: Framing differentiable read/write gating specifically as the mechanism for long-horizon credit-assignment in LM agents for text games, combined with the uncertainty-driven write/retrieval controls of UDDMC.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: Integrates known differentiable-memory advantages into a reward-driven LM context and prescribes concrete mechanisms for credit flow tied to surprise and utility gating.
references: Graves et al. (2016) Differentiable Neural Computer [differentiable memory]
Kaiser et al. (2017) Learning to remember rare events [memory and rare events]
Schaul et al. (2015) Prioritized Experience Replay [non-differentiable prioritization]

theory_name: Hierarchical Episodic-Semantic Memory (HESM) for Language-Model Agents
theory_description: A structured memory architecture that separates short-term working memory (recent tokens and actions), an episodic store of sparse, time-stamped experiences, and a semantic graph (grounded facts and abstractions). HESM prescribes consolidation rules (when and how episodic traces are abstracted into semantic graph nodes), retrieval arbitration across levels based on task-context and utility, and grounded alignment between textual episodes and semantic nodes so that LMs can generalize past episodic outcomes to novel but structurally similar quests.

law.theory_statement: An episodic trace e is consolidated into a semantic node if (A) its observed utility across episodes exceeds U_thresh (average episodic return contribution), and (B) its structural form recurs across k >= k_min distinct episodes or can be abstracted via schema induction; consolidation is accompanied by a linking mechanism storing provenance pointers to representative episodic traces.
law.domain_scope: Applies during offline or low-activity phases (analogous to sleep/replay) or during explicit consolidation steps between episodes. Requires episodic metadata (counts, return estimates) and a mechanism for structural abstraction (clustering, pattern mining).
special_cases: Single highly valuable one-off episodes may be preserved in ES but may not be consolidated if recurrence is absent; however, an override can consolidate if estimated utility is extremely high.
Noisy environments with spurious recurrence can create misleading semantic nodes; require statistical significance filtering or human-in-the-loop validation.

supporting_evidence.text: Neuroscience evidence of offline replay and selective consolidation of memories with behavioral relevance.
ML approaches that mine frequent patterns or cluster episodic traces can produce reusable subroutines/abstractions improving generalization.
Experience replay and offline RL use aggregated statistics across experiences to update global policies and value functions—consolidation mirrors this aggregation for memory abstraction.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Consolidation ideas (replay, clustering frequent patterns) exist in neuroscience and ML.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: Explicit dual-threshold (utility + recurrence) consolidation rule with provenance linking for LM agents to ensure traceability and replay-based reification of semantic nodes.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: Translates abstract consolidation concepts into actionable rules for LM-based agents operating on textual episodic traces.
references: McClelland et al. (1995) Complementary Learning Systems [consolidation/replay]
Sutton & Barto (2018) Reinforcement Learning [aggregating experiences to learn value]
Ravula et al. (2021) Schema induction works [pattern abstraction]

theory_name: Hierarchical Episodic-Semantic Memory (HESM) for Language-Model Agents
theory_description: A structured memory architecture that separates short-term working memory (recent tokens and actions), an episodic store of sparse, time-stamped experiences, and a semantic graph (grounded facts and abstractions). HESM prescribes consolidation rules (when and how episodic traces are abstracted into semantic graph nodes), retrieval arbitration across levels based on task-context and utility, and grounded alignment between textual episodes and semantic nodes so that LMs can generalize past episodic outcomes to novel but structurally similar quests.

law.theory_statement: At query-time, retrieval arbitration scores candidate items from each tier using a utility-weighted combination: Score = alpha(context_relevance) * Beta_tier * Importance, where Beta_tier encodes preferred tier given task horizon (e.g., WM high for immediate next-token prediction; ES preferred for episodic recall; SG favored for generalized planning); a learned gating network chooses alpha and Beta_tier conditioned on LM state and uncertainty.
law.domain_scope: Applies when multiple tiers are present and retrieval budgets require choosing which tier to draw from. The arbitration mechanism must be differentiable when end-to-end training is used.
special_cases: When WM contains sufficient tokens, arbitration can defer to WM and ignore ES/SG to save retrieval tokens.
When SG contains conflicting or outdated facts, arbitration must prefer recent ES traces or use provenance for disambiguation.

supporting_evidence.text: Hybrid memory systems in AI often require a controller to decide which memory to consult based on context and cost (e.g., gating in hierarchical caches).
In human cognition, strategic retrieval draws from different memory systems depending on task demands (episodic recall vs. semantic knowledge).

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Memory arbitration/controllers are known in systems architectures and cognitive models.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: A concrete parameterization (Beta_tier and learned alpha) tailored to LM agent state and uncertainty, designed specifically to manage token-budgeted retrieval across WM/ES/SG in text-game agents.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: Implements known arbitration within the specific constraints of LMs and text games, providing an operational approach to tier selection.
references: Sukhbaatar et al. (2015) Memory Networks [memory controllers]
McClelland et al. (1995) Complementary Learning Systems [systems interaction]

theory_description: Agents augment transformer language models with a compact, learned predictive transition model stored in memory (a world-model memory). This memory stores generalized transition primitives (compressed state-action -> predicted next-observation / latent_delta) that an LM can query to simulate outcomes, enabling multi-step lookahead, counterfactual reasoning, and sample-efficient planning in text games without full environment interaction.

law.theory_statement: Given predictive memory M with per-step prediction error e (expected distance between predicted and true next observation in an observation-similarity metric), the expected error of an n-step simulated rollout grows approximately as E_rollout(n) = O(n^p * e) with p in [0.5,1] in typical structured text games (p<1 because symbolic constraints and canonical outcomes correct cumulative drift); the agent's effective planning depth is the largest n such that E_rollout(n) remains below a task-dependent planning-threshold T_plan.
law.domain_scope: Text games with structural constraints (objects, canonical verbs, deterministic or low-entropy outcomes) where an observation-similarity metric (e.g., semantic embedding distance or token-overlap) meaningfully correlates with downstream decision quality.
special_cases: If the game contains branching hidden state not recoverable from text outputs, error may grow linearly or faster (p≈1).
If memory M includes symbolic constraints or invariants (e.g., object-ownership algebra), p can be <0.5 for some environments.

supporting_evidence.text: Empirical work on learned simulators shows cumulative model error grows with rollout length; however, structural priors and symbolic constraints can mitigate drift.
Many text games enforce invariants (e.g., an object cannot be in two places simultaneously), which can be exploited by a learned model to reduce error growth.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Model-based RL analyses of compounding model error in rollouts are established; world-model work formalizes error accumulation.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: Applying an explicit approximate scaling law for rollout error in text-game language-space and connecting it to an LM's effective planning depth via a task threshold T_plan.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: Extends existing model-error analyses into the textual / symbolic domain and makes testable predictions about planning depth.
references: Ha & Schmidhuber (2018) 'World Models' [model-based rollouts, error accumulation]
Côté et al. (2018) 'TextWorld' [structured text-game mechanics]

theory_description: LM agents should maintain a structured memory with two cooperative layers: (1) episodic traces (detailed transcripts of recent interactions) and (2) compressed semantic abstractions (generalized facts, rules, object affordances). Retrieval policies mediate between them: episodic retrieval for exact replays and recovery, semantic retrieval for generalization and planning. Together these layers enable both one-shot recovery from surprises and transfer across similar games.

law.theory_statement: Frequent co-occurrences across episodic traces are compressed into semantic facts (e.g., 'turning on lamp yields light in room') via an abstraction operator A that maps sets of aligned transcripts to canonicalized rules; the rate of successful compression depends on repetition frequency f and semantic-salience s such that probability of abstraction P_abstract ≈ 1 - exp(-k * f * s) for some k>0.
law.domain_scope: Text games and interactive text environments where repeated patterns occur across episodes (multiple runs or different rooms/scenarios) and where transcripts can be aligned into templated event structures.
special_cases: Singleton events with high importance (rare but critical) may be missed by frequency-based compression and require dedicated attention mechanisms.
Spurious co-occurrences due to coincidental language patterns may produce incorrect abstractions if not validated.

supporting_evidence.text: Human memory research distinguishes episodic traces from semantic knowledge; repeated episodic experiences are known to form semantic memory.
Engineering systems (e.g., learning from demonstrations and pattern mining) successfully extract rules from repeated trajectories.
In text games, many local rules recur across contexts (e.g., light sources enable inspection), enabling abstraction.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Cognitive theories of episodic-to-semantic consolidation and ML work on rule extraction from trajectories exist.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: A quantitative, operational compression law P_abstract ≈ 1 - exp(-k*f*s) applied to LM-agent memory consolidation in text games, connecting repetition and salience to abstraction probability.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: Applies cognitive consolidation intuition to LM memory design with a simple testable functional form.
references: Tulving (1972) 'Episodic and semantic memory' [cognitive foundation]
Blundell et al. (2016) 'Neural Episodic Control' [episodic storage for RL]
Graves et al. (2016) 'Differentiable Neural Computer' [structured memory systems]

theory_description: LM agents should maintain a structured memory with two cooperative layers: (1) episodic traces (detailed transcripts of recent interactions) and (2) compressed semantic abstractions (generalized facts, rules, object affordances). Retrieval policies mediate between them: episodic retrieval for exact replays and recovery, semantic retrieval for generalization and planning. Together these layers enable both one-shot recovery from surprises and transfer across similar games.

law.theory_statement: At decision time, the agent chooses retrieval from episodic memory when the uncertainty over current state U_state (measured by entropy over plausible states) exceeds a threshold U_e; otherwise it prefers semantic memory retrieval. This balances exact recovery (episodic) and generalization (semantic) and minimizes expected decision-time error.
law.domain_scope: Applies to transformer LM agents with access to both detailed transcripts and compressed semantic facts, where a computational budget for retrieval is constrained and uncertainty estimates are available (e.g., via LM calibration or ensemble spreads).
special_cases: If uncertainty estimates are unreliable, retrieval decisions may misroute, causing failures.
When episodic memory is too large to search efficiently, a pre-filtering or indexing step is required.

supporting_evidence.text: Episodic memory improves recovery of rare events; semantic memory supports generalization. Hybrid retrieval strategies have been effective in other agent contexts.
Uncertainty-based retrieval (querying detailed logs when uncertain) is an established heuristic in decision systems and active learning.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Hybrid memory architectures and uncertainty-driven querying exist in RL and question-answering systems.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: Formalizing a decision rule that switches between episodic and semantic memory retrieval based on a measurable current-state uncertainty metric for LM agents in text games.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: Synthesis of existing memory ideas into a clear retrieval policy suited for LMs acting in textual environments.
references: Blundell et al. (2016) 'Model-Free Episodic Control' [episodic value storage]
Graves et al. (2016) 'Differentiable Neural Computer' [memories accessed by controller]

theory_description: LM agents playing text games maintain a two-tier memory: a short-term, token-level working context (sliding window + attention cache) and a compressed episodic-context memory that stores structured summaries of past episodes (summaries include key locations, persistent object states, failed/successful action sequences, and discovered subgoals). The agent dynamically promotes frequently-relevant episodic entries into a mid-term deliberation buffer used for planning and retrieval, and demotes or consolidates infrequent entries. This hierarchy balances the LM's limited attention with long-term task-relevant memory, enabling multi-episode strategic behavior, subgoal chaining, and efficient reuse of past solutions.

law.theory_statement: Maintain (1) a working context W as the LM's immediate attention span (token-limited sliding window plus persistent attention cache) for fluent language-grounded decision-making, and (2) an episodic-context memory E that stores compact structured summaries S_k of past episodes k. Retrieval for decision d_t is an attention-weighted soft combination Retrieval(d_t) = alpha(t) * read_W(d_t) + (1 - alpha(t)) * read_E(d_t), where alpha(t) adapts based on novelty and recency signals (alpha in [0,1]).
law.domain_scope: Applies to language-model-based agents interacting in partially-observable, sequential text environments (interactive fiction/text games) where episodic experiences across episodes or long horizons contain reusable information and LM attention is limited (< ~8k tokens).
special_cases: In extremely short-horizon tasks (< W length) E provides little benefit and alpha ~ 1.
When the environment is adversarially stochastic, over-reliance on E can mislead; alpha should adapt toward W.
If summaries S_k are lossy or misaligned with decision needs, retrieval harms performance.

supporting_evidence.text: Transformer LMs have limited effective context windows; augmenting with episodic memory improves long-horizon tasks (general literature on memory-augmented neural networks and Transformer-XL style recurrence).
Human episodic memory is hierarchical (working vs. long-term) and supports planning via retrieval of past episodes; this is an inspiration for architectural division.
Agents that summarize and reuse past trajectories (experience replay, episodic control) improve sample efficiency in RL and sequential tasks.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Memory-augmented networks and experience replay separate short- and long-term information; transformer recurrence and retrieval-augmented generation use similar read mechanisms.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: Explicit alpha-weighted, novelty-modulated interpolation between working context reads and structured episodic summary reads tailored for LM agents in text games, plus the operationalization of promotion/demotion consolidation rules for episodic entries.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: HECM synthesizes existing memory and retrieval ideas but provides a task-specific, operational hierarchy and an adaptive interpolation mechanism for LM decision-making in text games.
references: Graves et al. (2014) Neural Turing Machines [memory-augmented networks]
Dai et al. (2019) Transformer-XL [extended context via recurrence]
Weston et al. (2014) Memory Networks [episodic-style retrieval]
Côté et al. (2018) TextWorld [text-game benchmark and motivation]

theory_description: LM agents playing text games maintain a two-tier memory: a short-term, token-level working context (sliding window + attention cache) and a compressed episodic-context memory that stores structured summaries of past episodes (summaries include key locations, persistent object states, failed/successful action sequences, and discovered subgoals). The agent dynamically promotes frequently-relevant episodic entries into a mid-term deliberation buffer used for planning and retrieval, and demotes or consolidates infrequent entries. This hierarchy balances the LM's limited attention with long-term task-relevant memory, enabling multi-episode strategic behavior, subgoal chaining, and efficient reuse of past solutions.

law.theory_statement: Represent episodic summaries S_k with meta-features (frequency f_k, recency r_k, utility u_k measured by subsequent success-rate improvement) and consolidate memory by promoting entries where utility*u_weight + frequency*f_weight + recency*r_weight > threshold T_promote into a mid-term deliberation buffer D; demote or compress entries with utility < T_demote. Properly tuned weights produce sublinear growth of active deliberation set |D| relative to total episodes N (|D| = O(log N) under naturalistic tasks with repetitive structure).
law.domain_scope: Applies to agents that (a) generate compact structured summaries for episodes, (b) can track simple statistics (f_k, r_k, utility u_k) over episodes, and (c) operate in domains with recurring structure (e.g., locations/items/mechanics recurring across episodes).
special_cases: In procedurally-generated games with low recurrence, |D| may grow near-linearly and consolidation thresholds must adapt.
Utility estimation can be noisy early in training; bootstrap safeguards are required to avoid discarding rare-but-critical episodes.

supporting_evidence.text: Experience replay and prioritized replay in RL use priority signals (TD error, success) to select experiences; consolidation improves sample efficiency.
Biological memory consolidation prioritizes salient, frequently reactivated episodes; inspiration for promotion/demotion heuristics.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Prioritized experience replay and memory consolidation heuristics exist in RL and neuroscience-inspired ML.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: Applying a compact, parametric promotion-demotion law to LM-generated episodic summaries with explicit predicted sublinear active-set scaling in text-game environments.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: This law is an adaptation of prioritized replay ideas into the episodic-summary domain with an explicit predicted complexity bound.
references: Schaul et al. (2016) Prioritized Experience Replay [prioritization of replayed experiences]
Graves et al. (2014) Neural Turing Machines [memory operations]
Côté et al. (2018) TextWorld [motivating text-game memory needs]

theory_description: LM agents should condition memory retrieval on candidate actions: when evaluating an action a, retrieve only those episodic summaries and substructures that are action-relevant (e.g., contain same objects, locations, verbs). Retrieved contexts are then synthesized via the LM into an imagined successor observation distribution. Action-conditioned retrieval reduces noise, improves model-based rollouts, and enables counterfactual evaluation of candidate action sequences without full environment execution.

law.theory_statement: For candidate action a_t, compute a relevance score R(S_k, a_t) between action and episodic summary using a learned scorer (embedding dot-product or LM-scored relevance). Retrieve top-K summaries with highest R and use them as additional context for action evaluation. This targeted retrieval yields higher precision of recalled actionable facts than blind retrieval of top-K by topical similarity.
law.domain_scope: Applies to LM agents that can (a) enumerate candidate actions or action templates, (b) embed actions and summaries into a common relevance space, and (c) perform LM-conditioned synthesis with retrieved contexts.
special_cases: If candidate action set is extremely large, scoring cost becomes a bottleneck; hierarchical candidate filtering is required.
If relevance scorer is trained on biased data, retrieval may systematically ignore rare but correct summaries.

supporting_evidence.text: Retrieval-augmented generation improves downstream tasks when retrieval is relevant; conditional retrieval (query-aware) outperforms unguided retrieval in many NLP tasks.
Action-conditioned world models in RL (action-conditional prediction) provide better rollouts than action-agnostic models.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Retrieval-augmented LMs and action-conditional world models are known independently.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: Combining action-conditioned retrieval of episodic summaries with LM-based synthesis for counterfactual rollouts specifically in text-game agents, and claiming precision gains versus topical retrieval.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: ACRS integrates action-conditioned retrieval into LM agent rollouts; this is a targeted adaptation of retrieval-augmented generation and action-conditional world models.
references: Lewis et al. (2020) Retrieval-Augmented Generation [RAG style retrieval]
Ha & Schmidhuber (2018) World Models [action-conditional rollouts]
Weston et al. (2014) Memory Networks [conditional read]

theory_description: LM agents should condition memory retrieval on candidate actions: when evaluating an action a, retrieve only those episodic summaries and substructures that are action-relevant (e.g., contain same objects, locations, verbs). Retrieved contexts are then synthesized via the LM into an imagined successor observation distribution. Action-conditioned retrieval reduces noise, improves model-based rollouts, and enables counterfactual evaluation of candidate action sequences without full environment execution.

law.theory_statement: Let p_hat be the LM-conditioned predicted success probability per simulated step for candidate rollouts using retrieved contexts, and c_sim be cost (compute) per simulated step. If p_hat > p_env * (c_env / c_sim) where p_env is baseline empirical success-per-step in environment and c_env is environment interaction cost, then counterfactual evaluation reduces expected real-environment interactions required to find successful plans.
law.domain_scope: Applies to scenarios where simulation cost is meaningfully cheaper than environment interactions, and the LM's synthesized successor distributions produce informative predictions (p_hat reasonably correlated with real outcomes).
special_cases: If synthesized predictions are uncorrelated with environment outcomes, p_hat is unreliable and the inequality fails; counterfactual rollouts then mislead.
For extremely cheap environment steps (e.g., deterministic local simulator), simulation advantage disappears.

supporting_evidence.text: Model-based RL theory shows benefit when model predictions are accurate relative to environment costs; LM-based synthesis acts as a learned model.
Empirical work on planning with learned models shows reduction in environment interactions when model-step accuracy is sufficient.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Model-based RL provides cost-benefit analyses for planning vs. executing; retrieval-augmented LMs have been used for simulation-like tasks.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: Applying a concrete inequality binding predicted per-step success probability, simulation vs. environment costs, and LM-action-conditioned retrieval for text-game agents.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: This statement formalizes when LM-based counterfactual rollouts help; it adapts established model-based cost bounds to LM-retrieval synthesis in text games.
references: Ha & Schmidhuber (2018) World Models [model-based planning]
Sutton (1991) Dyna Architecture [planning with learned models]
Lewis et al. (2020) Retrieval-Augmented Generation [retrieval improves generation]

theory_description: For long-horizon, low-stochasticity text-adventure games requiring deep planning, a sparse key-value episodic memory with constrained capacity and narrow retrieval (top-k small) yields higher task success than extending the transformer's dense attention context beyond practical limits. The mechanism is reduced attention noise and preserved salient episodic structure through compact indexed retrieval.

law.theory_statement: Fork: For deterministic or low-stochasticity text games with required planning depth T_planning >= 500 steps, an episodic-indexing memory (key-value store capacity C in [64,512], top-k retrieval k = 8) will outperform extending the LM dense attention context window beyond W = 2048 tokens. Numeric thresholds: T_planning >= 500, W_threshold = 2048 tokens, episodic capacity C ∈ [64,512], top-k k = 8. Negative prediction: Increasing the dense attention window to W >= 8192 tokens will NOT increase mean success rate relative to the episodic-indexing agent and will reduce success if episodic capacity C < 64 or top-k k > 32.
law.domain_scope: Deterministic or low-stochasticity (per-step transition entropy < 1.0 nat) single-player text-adventure games with required planning depth >= 500 steps, branching factor B ∈ [2,6], running on transformer LMs with baseline context capacity 2048 tokens and parameter count between 100M and 10B.
special_cases: Observations whose single-step tokenized length > 2048 tokens (single-observation huge blobs) — episodic keys that summarize such blobs could lose necessary detail and fail.
Environments with per-step transition entropy >= 2.0 nats (high stochasticity) where past episodic traces poorly predict future transitions.
Tasks that require remembering continuous numeric values or counters with fine precision across >10k steps — compact embedding keys may quantize away required precision.

supporting_evidence.text: background_principle: Dense self-attention cost and interference scale with token count; retrieving a small set of salient items from a key-value index avoids quadratic attention to all tokens.

inference: For planning horizons >> context window length, repeated retrieval of temporally-distant salient states via compact indices preserves decision-relevant information without linear growth in attention computation.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Existing work proposes both extending transformer context and using external memory; prior comparisons are qualitative or lack concrete numeric cross-over thresholds.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: Concrete numeric cross-over claim (planning depth >=500 favors episodic-indexing; context window W>=8192 will not beat episodic-indexing under stated capacity/top-k bounds) and an explicit negative prediction about large context windows degrading performance under capacity constraints.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: Extends prior memory-vs-context work by committing to numeric thresholds and a falsifiable comparative experiment.

law.discriminating_experiment: Take a fixed pretrained 1B-parameter transformer LM (no architecture change to model internals). Build two agents: (A) Episodic-indexing with capacity C=256 entries, key dim 128, top-k=8 retrieval, write-on-event novelty policy; (B) Extended-context with no external memory but transformer context window W=8192 tokens (all other training/data equal). Evaluate both on 100 procedurally-generated deterministic text games with average required planning depth >=500 and branching factor ≈3. Metric: mean success rate across games; run 30 random seeds per game. Compare means with paired test.
law.falsifies_if: Agent (B) with extended context W >= 8192 achieves an absolute >10 percentage-point higher mean success rate than agent (A) across the evaluation suite (p < 0.05 by paired test).

theory_name: Compressed Semantic Memory for Goal Conditioning
theory_description: When language-model agents play long, highly-branching text games under tight memory-bytes budgets, storing compressed semantic summaries (fixed-size learned embeddings per event) and maintaining them with reservoir sampling plus periodic compression yields higher goal-directed success than storing raw token sequences. The compression removes irrelevant lexical variation and increases retrieval precision for goal-conditioned policies when compression exceeds a specific ratio.

law.theory_statement: Fork: For text-adventure environments with branching factor B >= 5 and mean episode length L_avg >= 200, an embedding-based compressed memory (embedding dimension D = 64) with effective compression ratio R >= 16 (stored memory bytes <= 1/16 of raw-token bytes) outperforms raw-token episodic replay under the same memory-byte budget; numeric thresholds: B = 5, L_avg = 200, D = 64, R = 16; negative prediction: under these conditions raw-token replay will NOT outperform the compressed embedding memory and, if compression ratio is reduced below R = 4, compressed-memory performance will degrade relative to the R >= 16 case; discriminating experiment: build a suite of procedurally-generated English-like text games with B = 6 and L_avg = 400, fix memory budget to 1 MB, and compare (A) an agent that stores D = 64 embeddings sampled by reservoir sampling and periodically compresses to maintain R >= 16, against (B) an agent that stores raw tokens until the same budget is exhausted; measure mean success rate across 50 games × 20 seeds and retrieval-precision (human-annotated relevance of retrieved items); falsifier: if agent B (raw-token) attains >=5 percentage points higher mean success rate than agent A across the suite (p < 0.05), this statement is falsified.
law.domain_scope: Procedurally-generated English-like text-adventure games with branching factor >= 5, mean episode length >= 200 steps, memory budget in the range 0.5–5 MB, and decision-making by a language model that conditions on retrieved memories.
special_cases: Games that require exact string reproduction (single-use passphrases, exact codes) where an embedding cannot recover the exact token sequence; compressed-memory may fail.
Very low-branching environments (B <= 2) where raw-token storage retains high signal and compression provides no advantage.

supporting_evidence.text: background_principle: Fixed-size semantic embeddings (e.g., 64-d) cluster semantically similar states and remove lexical noise, improving nearest-neighbor retrieval precision under constrained storage.

inference: As branching factor increases, the relevance-signal-to-noise ratio of stored raw-token contexts falls faster than the compressible semantic content does; therefore high compression that preserves semantics raises effective retrieval SNR when bytes are limited.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Embedding-based memory and reservoir sampling have been proposed for language agents.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: Concrete numeric thresholds (B >= 5, L_avg >= 200, D = 64, R >= 16), an explicit negative prediction against raw-token replay under equal memory-bytes, and a concrete falsifier bound (>=5% advantage to raw-token refutes).
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: Extends existing memory-compression ideas with specific, testable regime bounds and a discriminating experiment.

law.discriminating_experiment: Create 50 procedurally-generated text games with branching factor 6 and average length 400. Fix agent LM policy architecture and memory-byte budget at 1 MB. Agent A: store D = 64 embeddings with reservoir sampling and periodic PCA/quantization to maintain storage <= 1/16 of raw bytes. Agent B: store raw tokens until 1 MB. Use identical retrieval mechanism and decision-conditioning. Run 20 random seeds per game. Report mean success rate, retrieval precision (human-annotated), and median episode reward.
law.falsifies_if: Raw-token agent B obtains >=5 percentage points higher mean success rate than compressed-embedding agent A across the suite (p < 0.05).

theory_description: When text games contain repeated multi-step subroutines, inducing and storing 'schemas' (macro-actions) that replace sequences of primitive actions with single macro tokens yields superior long-horizon planning compared to flat episodic or compressed-memory approaches, provided macro induction captures >= 20% of trajectory steps and macro length k >= 4.

law.theory_statement: Fork: For procedurally-generated text games with branching factor B >= 8 and average episode length L_avg >= 400 that contain repeated subroutines, a schema-based memory that identifies and stores macros of median length k >= 4 and that compresses >= 20% of trajectory tokens into macros will outperform both compressed-embedding memory and raw-token episodic replay when schema vocabulary size V_schema <= 500 and memory budget <= 5 MB; numeric thresholds: B = 8, L_avg = 400, k = 4, compress >= 20%, V_schema = 500, budget = 5 MB; negative prediction: under these conditions neither compressed embeddings nor raw-token replay will achieve higher mean success; discriminating experiment: generate games with engineered repeated subroutines making up exactly 25% of steps, run three agents with identical LM decision policies and memory budgets (5 MB): (A) schema-agent that discovers macros online (merge-subsequence heuristic + frequency threshold) and stores macros as single tokens, (B) compressed-embedding agent (D = 128, R = 8), (C) raw-token replay agent; measure mean success rate and planning depth; falsifier: if either agent B or C attains >=7 percentage points higher mean success than schema-agent A across the suite, the statement is falsified.
law.domain_scope: Procedurally-generated English-like text-adventure games that include repeated multi-step subroutines (deterministic or stochastic), branching factor >= 8, mean episode length >= 400, memory budgets up to 5 MB, and language-model decision policies capable of conditioning on macro-tokens.
special_cases: Games where repeated subsequences are semantically brittle (small lexical changes alter outcomes) and so macros cause harmful overgeneralization.
Games where the cost of discovering macros online exceeds the compute/time budget and so macros cannot be reliably induced.

supporting_evidence.text: background_principle: Hierarchical reinforcement learning (options, macro-actions) reduces effective horizon and improves planning in environments with repeated subroutines.

inference: Many procedurally-generated text games include repeated 'fetch' or 'unlock' subroutines; compressing repeated subsequences into single tokens reduces branching at the planning level and increases effective sample efficiency when compression covers >= 20% of steps.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Options/skills and macro-actions in RL; chunking in sequence models.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: Numeric thresholds for branching, episode length, required compression fraction (>= 20%), macro length (k >= 4), schema vocabulary size, and a direct comparison to compressed embeddings under equal memory budgets.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: Specializes hierarchical-memory ideas to text-game agents with explicit falsifiers and experiment design.

law.discriminating_experiment: Construct a suite of 40 procedurally-generated games where repeated subroutines occupy 25% of steps and branching = 8. Fix memory budget = 5 MB and LM policy. Agent A: online macro induction (frequency threshold 0.02, merge if substitute reduces average token length by >=4), store macros as single tokens with schema vocab cap V_schema = 500. Agent B: compressed embeddings D = 128, R = 8. Agent C: raw-token replay. Run 30 seeds per game. Compare mean success rate, planning depth (average macro uses per episode), and failure modes.
law.falsifies_if: Either compressed-embedding agent B or raw-token agent C achieves >=7 percentage points higher mean success rate than schema-agent A (p < 0.05) across the suite.

theory_description: Agents should allocate a fixed number of high-priority memory 'goal slots' separate from a larger episodic store; above a concrete slot-count threshold these slots enable chaining of subgoals and reduce interference, improving long-horizon quest completion at the cost of some short-term reactivity.

law.statement_name: Slot-count fork: prioritized slots vs recency-only
law.theory_statement: Fork: For text-game agents with total memory capacity M_total and episodic event-level entries, configuring K prioritized goal slots with K >= 128 improves long-horizon quest-completion rate compared to K <= 32; numeric threshold: K = 128 slots; negative prediction: setting K <= 32 will NOT improve long-horizon quest-completion (it will reduce completion rate by >=10% relative to K=128) for games with required plan-chain length >=3 and episode length >=300; discriminating_experiment: keep M_total constant, implement identical retrieval and scoring mechanisms, vary K in {16,32,128,512} and evaluate on 100 procedurally generated quests requiring plan-chain length 3-7 and episode length 300-800; measure quest-completion rate and average subgoal chaining length; falsifies_if: if K <= 32 yields >=10% higher quest-completion rate than K=128 across the evaluation suite (p<0.05).
law.domain_scope: Text-adventure games where (a) events are discrete steps (actions/observations) stored as entries, (b) tasks require chaining >=3 subgoals, and (c) total memory capacity M_total is fixed between 8k and 64k entries.
special_cases: Purely reactive micro-games with plan-chain length = 0 where prioritized slots are unnecessary.
Extremely long-world-knowledge tasks where relevant facts are immutable and should be stored in a separate persistent KB rather than goal slots.

supporting_evidence.text: background_principle: Fixed dedicated high-priority slots reduce interference between temporally local episodic traces and stable task-critical goals by creating a separate address space for high-utility items.

inference: When K is too small relative to required concurrent subgoals, goal slot eviction causes loss of intermediate targets and breaks chaining; increasing K above a threshold reinstates chaining capability.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Memory prioritization and dedicated buffers are used in some agents and cognitive models, but fixed numeric slot-count thresholds are rarely specified.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: An explicit fork committing that K >= 128 is necessary (vs K <= 32) for improved long-horizon chaining under constrained total capacity, producing a falsifiable numeric bound and prediction of a >=10% effect.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: Goes beyond general prioritization by prescribing slot-count and a discriminating experiment.

law.discriminating_experiment: Implement an LM-based agent with identical scoring and retrieval; set total store size to 16k event entries. Create four variants with K=16,32,128,512 dedicated goal slots. Generate 100 procedural quests requiring 3-7 chained subgoals and episode lengths 300-800. Evaluate quest completion and average intact subgoal chains.
law.falsifies_if: Any K <= 32 yields >=10% higher quest-completion rate than K=128 across the test suite (p<0.05).

theory_name: Sparse Landmark Anchoring and Low-frequency Replay (SLARF)
theory_description: Store only high-surprise 'landmark' events (top P percentile) and perform low-frequency scheduled replay; this sparse-anchor + low-replay regime enables efficient long-horizon planning by preserving salient connectors while avoiding retrieval noise from dense stores.

law.statement_name: Sparsity-replay fork: landmarks with low-frequency replay vs dense high-frequency replay
law.theory_statement: Fork: For text-game agents solving long-horizon tasks (episode length >=500) the regime 'store only top P=5% surprise events + replay frequency F = 0.02 per step' yields higher planning efficiency than 'store P >=20% + replay F >= 0.2 per step'; numeric thresholds: P=5% and F=0.02; negative prediction: increasing storage density to P >=20% and replay frequency to F >=0.2 will NOT increase planning efficiency and will reduce planning success per unit compute by >=12% relative to the sparse regime; discriminating_experiment: run agents with storage percentile P in {1%,5%,20%,50%} and replay frequency F in {0.00,0.02,0.2,0.5}; evaluate planning success, average retrieval latency, and planning-success-per-compute on 80 long-horizon games (episode length 500-1200) requiring bridging sparse connectors; falsifies_if: if any dense-high-replay configuration (P>=20% and F>=0.2) yields >=10% higher planning-success-per-compute than P=5%,F=0.02, the statement is falsified.
law.domain_scope: Text-adventure games with sparse salient connectors (critical events occur <5% of time), episode lengths >=500 steps, and agents constrained by limited retrieval compute budget per step.
special_cases: Games with uniformly informative steps where 'surprise' distribution is flat; landmark selection fails.
Environments where replay is free (no compute budget), making high-frequency replay viable.

supporting_evidence.text: background_principle: Storing and replaying only high-surprise events reduces noise and focuses learning/planning on connectors between sparse rewards.
inference: High-frequency replay over dense stores amplifies spurious correlations and increases retrieval cost per useful planning step, lowering planning efficiency.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Experience replay and prioritized replay are known in RL; sparse episodic landmarks are discussed in cognitive literature.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: Concrete sparse threshold P=5% combined with low-frequency replay F=0.02 and an explicit performance penalty for denser/higher-frequency regimes, forming a falsifiable fork.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: Combines percentile-based sparsity and absolute replay-frequency thresholds to create a discriminable experimental claim.

law.discriminating_experiment: Implement an LM-agent with identical surprise metric. Sweep P in {1,5,20,50}% and F in {0,0.02,0.2,0.5}. Run on 80 long-horizon procedurally generated games (500-1200 steps) needing sparse connectors. Measure planning-success-per-compute and retrieval latency.
law.falsifies_if: Any configuration with P>=20% and F>=0.2 achieves >=10% higher planning-success-per-compute than P=5%,F=0.02 across the suite (p<0.05).

theory_description: When branching factor is large, compressing episodic traces into hierarchical node summaries and connecting them into an explicit directed exploration graph yields superior planning ability over flat episodic key-value stores. The mechanism: graph topology preserves branching structure enabling targeted retrieval of alternative futures without exhaustive per-token attention.

law.theory_statement: Fork: For text games with branching factor B >= 4 and planning depth T between 100 and 2000 steps, a hierarchical summarization into graph nodes of max node-token-size N = 128 tokens with graph degree limit D = 8 (edges per node) and summarization cadence S = 10 steps will outperform a flat episodic key-value store with capacity C = 512 and top-k = 16. Numeric thresholds: branching factor B >= 4, node size N = 128 tokens, degree D <= 8, summarization cadence S = 10 steps, flat memory capacity C = 512, top-k = 16. Negative prediction: Increasing node-token-size to N >= 1024 will NOT increase mean success and will reduce performance by introducing noisy, high-variance node embeddings.
law.domain_scope: Single-player text-adventure games where branching factor B >= 4, planning depth T ∈ [100,2000], observation token lengths per step <= 512 tokens, and transformer LMs with baseline context 2048 tokens.
special_cases: Games where branching is shallow (B <= 2) — flat episodic memory or extended context may outperform graph memory.
Tasks where inter-node relationships are non-relational (pure sequence) rather than graph-like — graph structure adds overhead with no benefit.

supporting_evidence.text: background_principle: Explicit graph representations preserve topological branching relationships and allow targeted search algorithms (e.g., graph search) that do not require re-attending to entire token histories.
inference: Compressing contiguous segments into short node summaries (N=128 tokens) preserves sufficient decision-relevant detail for branching decisions while limiting attention and retrieval noise.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Some work stores episodic memory as summaries and others propose learned map- or graph-like memories, but numeric node-size, degree, and cadence thresholds are not standard.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: Specific numeric design: node-token-size N=128, degree cap D=8, summarization cadence S=10 steps and explicit negative prediction about N >= 1024 harming performance.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: Combines hierarchical summarization with an explicit graph topology and numeric bounds as a falsifiable design claim.

law.discriminating_experiment: Implement two agents on a suite of 80 procedurally-generated high-branching (B >= 4) text games with average depth T ≈ 500: (A) Graph-memory agent: summarize every S=10 steps into node summaries ≤128 tokens, link nodes with edges when transition observed, limit node out-degree to D=8, retrieve neighbors via targeted graph search; (B) Flat episodic agent: key-value store capacity C=512, top-k=16 retrieval, same LM backbone. Train/fine-tune equally. Evaluate mean success rate, mean number of irrelevant retrievals per retrieval, and planner call counts. 30 seeds per game.
law.falsifies_if: Flat episodic agent (B) achieves an absolute >10 percentage-point higher mean success rate than graph-memory agent (A) across the suite (p < 0.05), or graph-memory agent shows no statistically significant reduction in irrelevant retrievals.

theory_description: When environment-change events (inventory change, location change, new NPC encounter) occur sparsely relative to steps, restricting writes to event-triggered snapshots yields higher retrieval precision and downstream performance than continuous per-step writes for the same memory budget. The claim sets a concrete event-rate threshold below which event-triggered write strictly dominates continuous write.

law.theory_statement: Fork: For text games where environment-change event rate E_rate <= 0.25 (i.e., <=25% of steps are environment-change events) and average episode length L >= 100, an event-triggered write policy (write only on environment-change events) with deduplication will outperform continuous per-step write under equal memory budget; numeric thresholds: E_rate = 0.25, L = 100 steps; negative prediction: continuous per-step write will NOT outperform event-triggered write under these conditions and will reduce retrieval precision by >=10 percentage points; discriminating experiment: run two agents identical except write policy on a suite of games engineered to have E_rate=0.1 and L=200: (A) event-triggered write plus simple deduplication; (B) continuous per-step write until budget exhausted. Measure retrieval precision and success rate; falsifier: if continuous-write yields >=7% higher retrieval precision or success rate than event-write, the statement is falsified.
law.domain_scope: Text-adventure games where environment-change events are clearly defined and occur in <=25% of steps, average episode length >=100, and memory budgets constrained to small megabytes.
special_cases: Games with noisy or high-frequency event signals where event detection is unreliable; event-triggered policy may drop relevant micro-events.
Tasks where relevant temporal dynamics occur within non-event steps (e.g., a hidden timer that matters even without state-change events).

supporting_evidence.text: Background principle: Writing only salient events reduces redundant storage and focuses retrieval capacity on informative entries under constrained budgets.
Inference: When event_rate is low, continuous writes mostly add redundant near-duplicate states; deduplicated event writes increase effective information per stored entry.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Event-triggered and priority write policies have been suggested; continuous per-step write is a baseline.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: The concrete numeric event-rate threshold (E_rate <= 0.25), explicit deduplication requirement, and falsifiable negative prediction about continuous-write performance.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: Specifies operational thresholds where event-triggered write strictly dominates.

law.discriminating_experiment: Construct games with event_rate controlled at 0.1 and L=200. Compare two agents with identical LM and memory budget: (A) event-triggered writes on inventory/location/NPC change + deduplication, (B) continuous per-step writes until budget. Evaluate retrieval precision (human-annotated relevance) and success rate across 80 games with 20 seeds each.
law.falsifies_if: Continuous-write agent (B) attains >=7% higher retrieval precision or >=7% higher success rate than event-triggered agent (A) across the evaluation (p<0.05).

theory_description: Transformers implement exact, compositional integer arithmetic by learning low-dimensional linear place-value transforms applied iteratively across layers. There exists a sharp layer-depth-to-digit threshold: when model depth exceeds a numeric multiple of operand digit-length the model uses an algorithmic linear projection + carry propagation mechanism; when depth is below that multiple the model defaults to memorized mapping and fails algorithmic generalization.

law.statement_name: Layer-threshold fork for compositional linear arithmetic
law.theory_statement: Fork: For base-10 integer addition of two D-digit operands, if transformer depth L satisfies L >= 4 * D then Mechanism A (compositional linear arithmetic via learned linear projections and iterative carry propagation across layers) dominates; if L < 4 * D then Mechanism B (sparse memorized mapping of frequent input-output pairs) dominates. Numeric threshold: L = 4 * D. Negative prediction: For L >= 4 * D, held-out exact-addition accuracy on unseen D-digit pairs will NOT fall below 90% after standard training (see domain_scope); for L < 4 * D, held-out exact-addition accuracy will NOT exceed 60% without explicit example memorization of >50% of the input space. Discriminating experiment: For D in {2,4,6}, train transformer models (fixed width 1024, 16 heads) with depths L in {D,2D,3D,4D,5D} on a dataset containing 15% uniformly sampled D-digit addition pairs. Evaluate (1) exact-match accuracy on the held-out 85% of pairs, (2) exact-match accuracy on inputs with digit-place permutation (place-values shuffled), and (3) effect of replacing the final learned linear output projection with a random fixed linear map at inference. If models with L >= 4*D obtain >90% held-out exact accuracy, >85% on permuted-place tests, and drop >50 percentage points when final projection is randomized, Mechanism A is supported; otherwise the fork is false. Falsifier: Observing any model with L >= 4 * D that (a) after adequate training (converged training loss, >100k gradient steps for D>=4) has held-out exact-addition accuracy <= 90% on unseen D-digit pairs, or (b) shows <30 percentage-point accuracy drop when the final learned projection is randomized (indicating lookup-like output encoding), refutes this statement.
law.domain_scope: Decoder-only or encoder-decoder transformer architectures trained from scratch on exact base-10 integer addition of two equal-length operands. Operands are tokenized as single decimal digits (one token per digit), positional encodings present, model width in [512,4096], float16/float32 numerics, training dataset limited to <=20% of the D-digit input space so full memorization is infeasible. No chain-of-thought or explicit carry supervision provided during training. Applies to inference without stepwise prompting.
special_cases: If explicit stepwise/carry supervision is provided during training, the numeric multiplier (4) can reduce by up to 2x (i.e., L >= 2*D suffices).
If tokenization groups k digits per token, effective D is ceil(D/k) and the threshold uses that effective digit-length (see TPVE theory).

supporting_evidence.text: background_principle: Transformer depth enables multiple sequential transformations of position-wise embeddings; iterative computations can be implemented by repeated layer transforms.

inference: A minimal procedural decomposition of digit-wise addition requires at least four coarse pipeline steps per digit (read digit pair, compute partial sum, propagate carry, write output) motivating the approximately 4*D depth scaling.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Prior analyses show transformers can implement iterative computations and that depth correlates with algorithmic capacity; work exists on emergent arithmetic-like behaviors without an explicit numeric layer-to-digit rule.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: A concrete fork with a precise numeric threshold L = 4 * D, a negative prediction about held-out accuracy, and a single concrete ablation (final linear projection randomization) as a discriminating test between algorithmic and memorized solutions.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: Builds on existing qualitative ideas but commits to a falsifiable numeric boundary and concrete experiments.

law.discriminating_experiment: Train transformers (width 1024, 16 heads) on D-digit addition with D in {2,4,6} using 15% uniform coverage of the input space. For each D train models with depths L={D,2D,3D,4D,5D} to convergence. Evaluate exact-match on unseen 85% holdout, exact-match on place-value-permuted inputs, and accuracy after replacing the final learned linear output projection with a fixed random matrix. Support is: L>=4D models have >90% holdout accuracy, >85% permutation accuracy, and >50-point drop after projection randomization. Otherwise falsified.
law.falsifies_if: Any trained model with L >= 4 * D that (a) has held-out exact-match accuracy <= 90% given sufficient training, or (b) shows <30 percentage-point drop in exact-match accuracy when the final learned output projection is randomized, falsifies this statement.

theory_description: When training coverage of the mapping from inputs to outputs exceeds a coverage threshold, language models preferentially implement sparse memorized lookup encodings rather than algorithmic compositional arithmetic. This leads to near-perfect accuracy on seen pairs and sharply degraded generalization on unseen pairs; the switch point is a training-coverage numeric threshold independent of depth in the high-capacity regime.

law.statement_name: Coverage fork for memorization vs algorithmic arithmetic
law.theory_statement: Fork: For D-digit base-10 addition, let training coverage C be the fraction of all possible operand pairs seen during training. If C >= 0.25 then Mechanism B (sparse-memory lookup tables stored in embedding/output layers) dominates; if C < 0.25 then Mechanism A (algorithmic compositional arithmetic that generalizes to unseen pairs) dominates. Numeric threshold: C = 0.25 (25%). Negative prediction: For C >= 0.25, held-out exact-addition accuracy on unseen pairs will NOT exceed 55% despite near-perfect accuracy on seen pairs; for C < 0.25, held-out exact-addition accuracy on unseen pairs will NOT be below 85% for models meeting algorithmic capacity (e.g., depth >= 4*D per LLLA). Discriminating experiment: Train identical transformer models (width 1024, 16 heads, depth fixed at 4*D) on D-digit addition with datasets sampled at coverages C ∈ {0.05,0.15,0.25,0.4}. For each C measure: (1) exact-match accuracy on seen training pairs, (2) exact-match accuracy on the held-out unseen pairs, and (3) singular-value spectrum of the final token-output embedding matrix (to detect memorized lookup structure). If C >= 0.25 gives >98% seen accuracy but unseen accuracy <=55% and shows a low-rank plus sparse singular-value pattern consistent with memorized lookup, SMTL is supported. Falsifier: If models trained with C >= 0.25 routinely obtain >85% exact-match accuracy on unseen pairs without signs of sparse lookup embeddings (no large sparse singular values), or if models with C < 0.25 fail to reach >85% unseen accuracy given algorithmic capacity, this statement is falsified.
