law.domain_scope: Decoder-only transformers trained from scratch on base-10 D-digit addition, tokenized one digit per token, model depth set at 4*D to ensure algorithmic capacity per LLLA; training data coverage C measured as fraction of all ordered operand pairs seen during training; width between 512 and 4096; no chain-of-thought supervisory signals.
special_cases: If training includes heavy repetition and curriculum that emphasizes rare pairs, memorization threshold C can shift downward (i.e., lower coverage suffices for lookup).
When the model capacity (parameters) is orders of magnitude larger than the mapping cardinality, memorization can occur at lower C.

supporting_evidence.text: background_principle: Models with high parameter capacity can store many input-output associations in embedding and final-layer linear maps when those pairs are observed frequently.

inference: A sparse-memory implementation produces a characteristic embedding/output matrix with a few large singular values corresponding to memorized clusters and many small singular values, unlike distributed algorithmic implementations which produce smoother spectra.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Work on memorization in neural networks and dataset coverage influencing memorization vs generalization exists; prior descriptions are qualitative or capacity-based without a crisp 25% coverage fork for arithmetic.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: A specific numeric coverage threshold (25%) that predicts a sharp switch to lookup-based arithmetic and a concrete spectral discriminator for memorization.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: Translates general memorization principles into an explicit, falsifiable policy-relevant threshold and measurement.

law.discriminating_experiment: Train transformers (depth fixed at 4*D) on D-digit addition with coverages C={0.05,0.15,0.25,0.4}. Measure seen vs unseen exact-match, and compute SVD of final output embedding matrix to detect sparse-large singular values. Confirm memorization if C>=0.25 yields unseen <=55% and SVD shows sparse dominant singular values. Otherwise falsified.
law.falsifies_if: If models trained with C >= 0.25 achieve >85% exact-match on unseen pairs without spectral signatures of memorization, or if models with C < 0.25 fail to exceed 85% unseen accuracy when depth=4*D, this statement is falsified.

theory_description: Addition is implemented by repeated localized attention 'sweeps' across digit positions that sequentially propagate carries. Each sweep resolves carries across a contiguous window of W digit positions; the model needs S nonredundant attention-update sweeps to propagate carries across D digits, and correct algorithmic addition occurs only if S >= ceil(D / W). When S < ceil(D / W) the model cannot reliably propagate long carry chains and instead relies on memorized heuristics or statistical shortcuts.

law.theory_statement: Fork: For exact addition of two D-digit base-10 integers tokenized at single digits, if the model provides S attention-update sweeps and attention locality window W (digits) such that S >= ceil(D / W) then carry propagation proceeds algorithmically by localized sweeps (Mechanism A dominates); if S < ceil(D / W) then carry propagation fails systematically and the model relies on memorized or statistical heuristics (Mechanism B dominates). Numeric threshold: S = ceil(D / W). Negative prediction: If S >= ceil(D / W), accuracy on inputs requiring maximal-length carry chains (e.g., 999... + 1) will NOT depend on the frequency of those examples in training and will remain high (>90%) even when such examples occur at frequency <= 1e-5 of training samples. Discriminating experiment: Train decoder-only transformers (vocabulary = digits) on D = 12 digit addition with three enforced attention locality masks producing effective W in {1,2,4}; for each W train model depths L in {8,12,24} and operationalize S as the number of sequential layers producing >5% positional-update norm (measured by per-position embedding change). Make maximal carry examples appear at frequency 1 / 100000 in the training stream. Measure exact-match accuracy on held-out maximal-carry cases and on random holds. If runs with measured S >= ceil(D/W) achieve >90% exact-match on maximal-carry cases despite their rarity and runs with S < ceil(D/W) fail (<40% accuracy) the CSAM statement is supported. Falsifier: Any configuration with measured S >= ceil(D / W) that, after extensive training, fails to reach >90% exact-match on rare maximal-carry cases (or whose performance on those cases scales linearly with example frequency rather than with S) refutes this statement.
law.domain_scope: Decoder-only transformers trained for token-level (single-digit) exact integer addition in base-10, where attention locality can be enforced by sparse-masking; S is measured as the count of layers producing nontrivial cross-position update (operationalized via embedding-change norm). Applies to models without architectural recurrent modules and without multi-digit tokenization.
special_cases: If tokenization groups multiple digits per token (k>1) the effective D and W should be scaled by k; CSAM then applies to token-level positions instead of digit-level.
If a model uses global attention every layer (W = D), then S >= 1 suffices and CSAM reduces to a simple depth threshold.
If residual paths are surgically altered (e.g., removed or scaled), the measured S may change and the ceil(D/W) bound must be recomputed using the new update norm threshold.

supporting_evidence.text: background_principle: Hardware ripple-carry adders propagate carries sequentially across adjacent bits/digits; an analogous algorithmic requirement exists for any architecture constrained to local communication per update step.

inference: Attention implements the local communication channel in transformers; restricting attention locality W increases the number of sequential update steps required to move information across D positions, yielding the bound S >= ceil(D/W).

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Analogy between ripple-carry adders and sequential neural propagation, and prior work connecting sparse/local attention to algorithmic computation.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: An explicit, falsifiable quantitative sweep-count bound S >= ceil(D/W), a concrete negative prediction about robustness to rare maximal-carry examples, and a precise discriminating experiment manipulating W and S.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: Translates hardware adder intuition into an explicit transformer-attention bound that is directly testable and numerically specified.

law.discriminating_experiment: Train transformer decoders (embedding dim 512) on 12-digit addition with W enforced via sparse masks {1,2,4}. For each mask, train depths L in {8,12,24}. Define a layer as a sweep-step if mean L2 change of position embeddings after that layer >5% of initial embedding norm. Compute S as count of such layers. Insert maximal-carry examples at frequency 1/100k. Evaluate exact-match on maximal-carry held-out set and on random sums. Compare accuracy vs S and vs example frequency. Success pattern: accuracy >90% when S >= ceil(12/W) and <40% otherwise. Alternative patterns falsify CSAM.
law.falsifies_if: Measured S >= ceil(D/W) but model achieves <90% accuracy on rare maximal-carry cases after extensive training, or performance correlates strongly with example frequency and not S.

theory_description: Transformers can implement an associative parallel-prefix (scan) algorithm to compute carries in O(log D) sequential update layers by learning a local associative operator that combines partial-sum + carry information. If the model has depth L exceeding a small constant times log2(D) it will implement PPAS and compute carries in parallel; otherwise it cannot realize PPAS and will either use sequential sweeps or memorize.

law.statement_name: Log-depth fork for associative carry scan
law.theory_statement: Fork: For tokenized D-digit base-10 addition, if model depth L satisfies L >= 4 * ceil(log2(D)) then the model will implement a parallel-prefix associative-scan mechanism (Mechanism A dominates) and compute carries in O(log D) sequential layers; if L < 4 * ceil(log2(D)) then PPAS cannot be implemented and the model will not generalize to input lengths > D (Mechanism B dominates). Numeric threshold: L = 4 * ceil(log2(D)). Negative prediction: If L >= 4 * ceil(log2(D)), accuracy on exact addition will NOT degrade when D is increased up to 2^(L/4) (i.e., models generalize to lengths up to 2^(L/4)) without additional training. Discriminating experiment: Train models with identical width and tokenization on D=128 digit addition with depths L in {16,24,32,48}. For each L, evaluate zero-shot generalization exact-match on D' ∈ {128,256,512,1024}. If models with L >= 4 * ceil(log2(512)) = 4*9 = 36 obtain >90% exact-match on D'=512 and up to 2^(L/4), while models with L < 36 fail to generalize beyond D, PPAS is supported. Falsifier: If models with L >= 4 * ceil(log2(D)) systematically fail to generalize to longer D' up to 2^(L/4) (after sufficient training on D) or if successful generalization is only achievable by memorization of specific long examples, PPAS is falsified.
law.domain_scope: Decoder-only or encoder-decoder transformers trained on token-level (single-digit) exact integer addition in base-10; L counts decoder layers that perform inter-position mixing. Applies when model capacity (width) is sufficient to learn an associative binary operator in its layers.
special_cases: If models have insufficient width (e.g., hidden dimension << 128) to represent the associative operator, the L threshold must be increased or PPAS will fail.
If tokenization groups digits (k>1) the effective D should be rescaled by k and the threshold recomputed.

supporting_evidence.text: background_principle: Parallel-prefix (scan) algorithms compute associative aggregates in O(log N) steps by combining adjacent partial results using an associative operator; neural nets can learn associative operators in feedforward layers.

inference: A transformer can realize the associative operator using local layers that combine neighboring positions; repeated doubling (pairwise combination) across ~log2(D) stages suffices to propagate carries globally.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Known connection between associative scan algorithms and parallel architectures; prior work has suggested log-depth algorithmic implementations in neural models but rarely gave explicit depth thresholds tied to D.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: Concrete numeric depth threshold L >= 4 * ceil(log2(D)) with a direct prediction of zero-shot generalization range 2^(L/4).
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: Makes a risky, testable mapping from transformer depth to maximal zero-shot generalizable input length under a PPAS mechanism.

law.discriminating_experiment: Train transformer decoders (width 1024) on D=128 digit addition with depths L in {16,24,32,48}. After training to convergence on D=128, evaluate exact-match on D' = 256, 512, 1024 without fine-tuning. If models with L >= 36 show >90% exact-match on D'=512 and generalize up to 2^(L/4), and models with L < 36 do not, PPAS is supported.
law.falsifies_if: Models with L >= 4*ceil(log2(D)) fail to achieve >90% exact-match on longer D' up to 2^(L/4) after convergence on D, or if generalization to longer lengths occurs only when those exact longer-length examples were present in training.

theory_name: Sparse Algorithmic Alignment via Residual Scale (SAG-RS)
theory_description: Arithmetic algorithmic circuits in transformer language models emerge when the learned multiplicative scale applied to residual (skip) updates crosses a small positive threshold. Above that threshold, the model forms sparse, low-rank algorithmic modules that implement carry and digitwise operations; below it, the model instead stores dense memorized mappings and fails to generalize length or place shifts.

law.statement_name: Residual-scale fork for algorithmic vs memorized arithmetic
law.theory_statement: Fork: For transformer models trained from random initialization on exact integer addition tasks, if the learned mean absolute residual-scale parameter s_mean (mean over layers of the absolute multiplicative scale applied to the residual branch) is >= 0.03 then Mechanism A dominates: the model implements sparse, near-linear algorithmic modules enabling accurate generalization to sequences twice the training length; if s_mean <= 0.02 then Mechanism B dominates: the model implements dense memorized mappings and fails to generalize to double-length inputs. Numeric threshold: s_mean crossing between 0.02 and 0.03. Negative prediction: Models with s_mean <= 0.02 will NOT generalize to inputs of 2x training length and will show exact-match accuracy <= 30% on such longer inputs after standard training; models with s_mean >= 0.03 will NOT incur >10 percentage-point drop in exact-match accuracy when evaluated on 2x-length inputs compared to same-length held-out examples. Discriminating experiment: Train N=20 transformer models (identical architecture: L=12, width=768, same optimizer) with randomized initializations but with an additional learned multiplicative residual-scale parameter per layer initialized at values drawn to bias final s_mean either below 0.02 or above 0.03 (e.g., initialize from Uniform[0.005,0.015] vs Uniform[0.04,0.06]). Train on exact addition of two 6-digit integers with uniformly sampled digits. After convergence on held-out same-length test set, measure s_mean and evaluate exact-match on addition of two 12-digit integers (2x length). If models with s_mean >= 0.03 achieve >=90% of their same-length accuracy on 2x-length inputs while models with s_mean <= 0.02 drop to <=30% exact-match on 2x-length inputs, SAG-RS is supported. Falsifier: If, under the experiment above, models with s_mean <= 0.02 achieve >=70% of same-length accuracy on 2x-length inputs, or models with s_mean >= 0.03 fail to achieve >=70% of same-length accuracy on 2x-length inputs, then this statement is falsified.
law.domain_scope: Transformer encoder-decoder or decoder-only models trained from scratch (no arithmetic pretraining), trained on exact integer addition in base-10, architectures L in [6,24], width in [512,2048]. The residual-scale parameter is defined as an explicit learned multiplicative scalar applied to the residual connection per layer (if implementation lacks such a scalar, one must be added for measurement). Training data must be uniformly sampled digit-wise and provide no stepwise supervision.
special_cases: If a pretrained model already contains arithmetic-relevant structure from language data, the s_mean thresholds may shift upward; SAG-RS applies strictly to models trained from scratch on arithmetic.

If a model uses alternative normalization/residual designs (e.g., reversible layers without multiplicative residual scales), the scalar measure must be mapped to an equivalent gating metric before applying thresholds.

supporting_evidence.text: background_principle: Sparse, low-magnitude gating of information pathways permits modular subcircuits to form in deep networks by enabling selective credit assignment; multiplicative residual scaling is a plausible control variable for such sparsity.

inference: Larger residual-scale magnitudes amplify single-step transformations, making sequential algorithmic state transitions (like carry propagation) implementable with fewer layers; very small residual scales bias optimization toward direct pattern mapping (memorization).

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Prior work shows architecture components (residuals, gating) affect emergent algorithmic behavior, but no precise scalar threshold for residual-scale that predicts arithmetic generalization has been established.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: A precise, falsifiable numeric threshold for a measurable learned residual-scale parameter and an explicit discriminating experiment tying that threshold to length generalization.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: SAG-RS converts architecture-gating intuition into a measurable scalar predictor with a sharp fork and falsifier.

law.discriminating_experiment: Perturb initial residual-scale initializations to produce final s_mean clusters below 0.02 and above 0.03 across many runs; compare exact-match accuracy decay when evaluating on inputs of 2x training length. Success pattern described in the theory_statement supports SAG-RS.
law.falsifies_if: If in the discriminating experiment s_mean <= 0.02 models achieve >=70% of same-length accuracy on 2x-length inputs, or s_mean >= 0.03 models fail to achieve >=70% of same-length accuracy on 2x-length inputs.

theory_description: Arithmetic is implemented by attention heads that route carry information sparsely to a small set of downstream positions. When the effective number of attended positions per head (Neff = exp(H_attention)) is <= 3, carry routing circuits dominate and produce algorithmic carry propagation; when Neff >= 6, attention is diffuse and memorized mapping dominates.

law.statement_name: Attention sparsity fork for carry routing
law.theory_statement: Fork: For transformer models trained from scratch on base-10 integer addition, if the per-head effective attended positions Neff (exp of attention entropy, averaged over heads and informative token positions) is <= 3 then Mechanism A dominates: heads implement sparse routing enabling correct carry propagation across arbitrary distances; if Neff >= 6 then Mechanism B dominates: attention is diffuse, preventing reliable carry routing and causing failures on cross-boundary carries. Numeric thresholds: Neff <= 3 vs Neff >= 6. Negative prediction: Models with Neff <= 3 will NOT show systematic cross-token carry failures (exact-match on carry cases >=90%); models with Neff >= 6 will NOT exceed 40% exact-match on cross-token carry cases under identical training. Discriminating experiment: Train a population of identical transformers (L=16, width=1024) on two-operand additions up to 8 digits. After training, compute per-head Neff for positions near potential carry sites. Then (a) prune/zero out the top-K attention weights in heads to force Neff to drop below 3 and measure whether models that previously failed now generalize on cross-boundary carry tests; (b) conversely, apply random smoothing (softmax temperature increase) to raise Neff above 6 in models that previously succeeded and measure whether success degrades. If dropping Neff to <=3 recovers carry generalization and raising it above 6 destroys generalization consistently, CSAR is supported. Falsifier: If manipulating Neff as above does not change cross-boundary carry generalization (e.g., increasing Neff above 6 while preserving weights does not reduce carry success, or decreasing Neff to <=3 does not improve carry success), CSAR is falsified.
law.domain_scope: Transformer models trained from scratch on base-10 integer addition, measuring attention distributions from the softmax of scaled dot-product attention. Neff is computed over token positions excluding padding and averaged over heads and positions that are carry-relevant (digit positions). Training uses uniformly sampled digit combinations; no chain-of-thought supervision.
special_cases: If a model intentionally uses explicit positional carry registers (extra tokens reserved for carry), the relation to Neff changes; CSAR applies to models that implement carry via standard attention patterns between digit tokens.
In architectures with hard sparse attention mechanisms (top-k attention), Neff is discrete and thresholds must be interpreted accordingly (e.g., top-3 corresponds to Neff <=3).

supporting_evidence.text: background_principle: Attention heads that focus on a small set of positions can route information as discrete signals, enabling implementation of algorithmic wiring like carry propagation.
inference: High attention entropy (large Neff) distributes signal and prevents reliable per-position routing needed for sequential carry operations.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Prior analyses link specialized attention patterns to algorithmic behaviors; quantification of 'effective attended positions' as a predictor for arithmetic carry behavior has not been specified with numeric thresholds.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: A concrete numeric Neff fork (<=3 vs >=6) and an explicit causal manipulative experiment (prune/smooth attention) as a discriminating test.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: CSAR converts attention-focus intuition into a falsifiable sparsity rule with direct interventions.

law.discriminating_experiment: Prune top attention weights to force Neff <=3 in failing models and apply softmax temperature to increase Neff >=6 in succeeding models; measure effect on cross-boundary carry exact-match accuracy. Observed symmetric causal changes support CSAR.
law.falsifies_if: If manipulating Neff as described does not change cross-boundary carry performance (no consistent improvement when Neff <=3 or no consistent degradation when Neff >=6), CSAR is falsified.

theory_description: Transformer models implement integer addition either by executing an explicit sequential per-digit algorithm across depth (digit-local sequential layers) or by learning a parallel mapping/memorization that mixes digits immediately. The decisive tradeoff is model depth relative to digit-length: when depth L is large enough (>= 6*D) the model organizes computation into per-digit algorithmic steps along depth; when L < 6*D the model implements a parallelized, high-degree cross-position mapping that resembles memorization.

law.theory_statement: Fork: For exact addition of two base-10 integers with digit-length D (D >= 2), if model transformer depth L satisfies L >= 6 * D then the model implements a sequential per-digit algorithm across layers (Mechanism A dominates); if L < 6 * D then the model implements a parallel cross-position mapping/memorization (Mechanism B dominates). Numeric threshold: L_threshold = 6 * D. Negative prediction: For models with L >= 6*D, the fraction of attention mass from each token to non-adjacent digit-token positions will NOT exceed 0.15 on average across inference tokens (i.e., non-local attention mass <= 15%). Discriminating_experiment: Train a suite of transformer models with identical parameter counts and width but varying depth L in {3*D, 4*D, 5*D, 6*D, 8*D} on exact base-10 addition (same training data); at test time (held-out examples), (1) measure per-token attention matrices and compute average non-adjacent attention mass, (2) perform layer-ablation: remove/zero each layer i and record which digit positions' outputs degrade when that layer is ablated. DAL predicts that for L >= 6*D (a) non-adjacent attention mass <= 0.15 and (b) ablations will show sequential localized failures consistent with per-digit processing (ablating layer k will mainly damage digit floor(k/6) and higher-order digits processed later). Falsifier: If for models with L >= 6*D the measured non-adjacent attention mass > 0.30 (30%) or layer ablations produce immediate, uniform failure across all digits (no sequential localized pattern), DAL is falsified.
law.domain_scope: Decoder-only or encoder-decoder transformer architectures with fixed tokenization of single decimal digits, trained from scratch on exact base-10 integer addition tasks for digit-lengths D in [2,16]; model width and parameter count kept constant across depth variants; training to >95% exact-addition accuracy on held-out test sets; inference deterministic (no dropout).
special_cases: Models using multi-digit tokens (vocabulary-level multi-digit shortcuts) invalidate the per-digit token assumption; DAL then does not apply.
Very large models (>>10B parameters) may implement hybrid schemes that mix both mechanisms; DAL's strict L >= 6*D threshold applies to small-to-medium models (<=6B params).
If positional encoding is non-monotonic or learned in a way that bundles digits, DAL's layer-to-digit mapping may shift and require adapted thresholds.

supporting_evidence.text: background_principle: Algorithmic tasks with clear sequential dependencies (e.g., carry propagation) can be implemented either by time/depth-serial computation or by parallel unrolled mappings; depth provides capacity for sequentialization.

inference: Digit-wise carry is a sequential, low-entropy operation across positions; reproducing it across depth requires roughly O(D) sequential steps; empirically a factor of ~6 layers per digit fits observed layer-utilization patterns in some small transformers trained on algorithmic tasks (inference rather than citation).

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Prior work distinguishes algorithmic (sequential) solutions from memorization/parallel solutions for algorithmic tasks, and shows depth can enable sequentialization.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: A concrete numeric depth threshold L >= 6*D as a testable fork that predicts observable attention localization and layer-ablation signatures.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: Transforms qualitative depth vs algorithmic tradeoffs into a precise, falsifiable threshold and measurable attention/ablation signatures.

law.discriminating_experiment: Train multiple transformers with identical parameters except depth L in {3D,4D,5D,6D,8D}. Tokenize numbers into single-digit tokens. After convergence, (a) compute average per-token non-adjacent attention mass; (b) ablate layers one-by-one and record which digit outputs fail. DAL predicts non-adjacent attention mass <=0.15 and sequential digit-local ablation patterns for models with L >= 6*D. If observed, DAL supported.
law.falsifies_if: Observed non-adjacent attention mass >0.30 for models with L >= 6*D, or layer-ablation produces immediate uniform failure across digits (no sequenced localized errors) for those models.

theory_description: For arithmetic, transformer models prefer a representation in which per-step computation is dominated by iterated sparse linear operations (sparse attention + largely linear MLP influence) rather than dense nonlinear mixing. The decisive tradeoff is attention sparsity: when the fraction of nonzero attention entries per query row S <= 0.20 the model implements arithmetic via iterated sparse linear maps approximating modular addition; when S > 0.20 dense-nonlinear MLP interactions and memorization dominate.

law.theory_statement: Fork: For transformer-based addition on tokenized single digits, if average attention sparsity S (fraction of nonzero/large attention weights per query) satisfies S <= 0.20 then the model implements arithmetic primarily via iterated sparse linear transforms (Mechanism A dominates); if S > 0.20 then the model relies on dense nonlinear MLP-mediated memorization/mapping (Mechanism B dominates). Numeric threshold: S = 0.20. Negative prediction: For S <= 0.20 models, replacing MLP nonlinearities with identity (keeping learned linear maps) will NOT reduce exact-addition accuracy by more than 10 percentage points; for S > 0.20, the same replacement will reduce exact-addition accuracy by >30 percentage points. Discriminating_experiment: Train two groups of models: (A) with L1 regularization on attention logits to reach S <= 0.20, and (B) without such regularization yielding S > 0.20. After training, (1) replace all MLP nonlinearities (e.g., GeLU) with identity and re-evaluate exact-addition accuracy; (2) ablate sparse attention heads vs MLP layers to see which causes larger performance drop. ISLT predicts group A will retain >90% of baseline exact-addition accuracy after MLP linearization while group B will drop >30%. Falsifier: If group A (S <= 0.20) loses >25% absolute exact-addition accuracy upon MLP nonlinearization, or if group B retains >90% after linearization, ISLT is falsified.
law.domain_scope: Transformer architectures (decoder-only or encoder-decoder) trained on exact base-10 digit-tokenized addition tasks for digit-lengths D in [2,12]; attention sparsity measured as fraction of attention weights whose magnitude exceeds 10% of the max weight in their row (or equivalent threshold producing the same sparsity S); training to high accuracy (>95% exact-addition) with deterministic inference.
special_cases: Sparse attention enforced via architecture (e.g., local attention only) counts toward S; but models that achieve low S via trivial locality without carrying mechanism may be false positives unless they also preserve accuracy after MLP linearization.
If attention sparsity definition uses different thresholds, numeric S=0.20 should be reinterpreted with that thresholding rule.

supporting_evidence.text: background_principle: Linear iterative maps can exactly implement modular arithmetic when structured appropriately (e.g., via Toeplitz-like sparse matrices approximating digit shifts and carries).

inference: Enforcing sparsity biases the model toward implementing simple local interactions (carry between adjacent digits) which can be implemented with near-linear transforms; this makes MLP nonlinearities less essential.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Prior analyses show attention patterns can be sparse and that some algorithmic functions reduce to linear maps when represented in suitable bases.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: A concrete sparsity threshold S=0.20 that forks sparse-linear algorithmic implementation vs dense nonlinear memorization, plus the MLP-linearization ablation as a falsifiable test.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: Specifies how sparsity quantitatively determines whether arithmetic is implemented linearly vs nonlinearly.

law.discriminating_experiment: Train matched models with and without an attention L1 penalty to create groups with S<=0.20 and S>0.20. After training, replace MLP nonlinearities with identity and re-evaluate exact-addition accuracy; measure accuracy drop. ISLT predicts small drops (<10%) for the sparse group and large drops (>30%) for dense group. If observed, ISLT supported.
law.falsifies_if: If models with S <= 0.20 lose >25 percentage points of exact-addition accuracy when MLP nonlinearities are removed, ISLT is falsified; equivalently, if models with S > 0.20 retain >90% accuracy after MLP-linearization, ISLT is falsified.

theory_name: Hierarchical State Abstraction Through Episodic Memory Theory
theory_description: This theory posits that language model agents achieve effective performance in text games through a hierarchical memory system that maintains multiple levels of state abstraction. The agent encodes raw observations into episodic traces, constructs intermediate semantic representations of game state (locations, inventory, goals), and maintains high-level strategic knowledge. Memory retrieval is guided by relevance to current context, with different abstraction levels being accessed based on the planning horizon required. Performance improvements scale with the ability to maintain coherent cross-temporal state representations and selectively retrieve relevant past experiences. The theory applies primarily to scenarios where context length limitations or computational constraints prevent processing all historical observations directly, and where game complexity requires explicit state tracking beyond what can be maintained in the language model's internal activations alone.

law.theory_statement: Effective memory-augmented agents encode game experiences at multiple hierarchical levels: (1) raw textual observations, (2) parsed semantic state features (entities, locations, relations), and (3) abstract strategic knowledge (subgoal completion, causal chains). Performance on complex text games correlates positively with the number of abstraction levels maintained and the coherence of cross-level mappings.
law.domain_scope: Applies to language model agents (typically transformer-based models with 100M-70B parameters) playing multi-step text-based games with partially observable state, complex action spaces (>10 possible actions per step), and goals requiring strategic planning over 10+ steps. Most relevant for interactive fiction games (Zork, TextWorld, Jericho benchmarks) with spatial navigation, inventory management, and puzzle-solving elements where state cannot be fully inferred from a single observation.
special_cases: In very simple text games with full observability, linear narratives, and minimal planning requirements (<10 steps to goal), single-level encoding or direct language model inference may suffice without explicit memory structures.

Games with extremely large state spaces (>10,000 unique locations/objects) may require selective encoding where only task-relevant features are abstracted, potentially collapsing some hierarchy levels.
Real-time text games with strict latency constraints (<100ms per action) may require different trade-offs between encoding depth and processing speed, favoring shallower hierarchies.

For language models with very large context windows (>32K tokens) that can fit entire game trajectories, explicit hierarchical memory may provide diminishing returns compared to in-context learning, though this depends on the model's ability to effectively utilize long contexts.
Multi-agent cooperative text games may require additional hierarchy levels for modeling other agents' knowledge states and shared versus private information.

supporting_evidence.text: Transformer-based agents augmented with graph neural networks that maintain both raw text and structured relational state representations (nodes for entities/locations, edges for relationships) demonstrate 15-40% higher completion rates on TextWorld tasks compared to agents using only sequential text memory or graph memory alone.

Agents using explicit knowledge graph memory to represent spatial topology and object locations show 25-35% better navigation efficiency (measured by steps to reach target locations) than flat vector-based memory architectures in games like Zork and Adventure.

Memory architectures that explicitly track goal hierarchies with separate representations for high-level objectives (e.g., 'escape the dungeon') and low-level subgoals (e.g., 'find key', 'unlock door') achieve 20-30% higher success rates on long-horizon text game tasks (>100 steps) compared to flat goal representations.

Ablation studies on agents playing Jericho games show that removing intermediate semantic representations (entity extraction, relation parsing) causes 30-50% performance drops, larger than removing either raw observation history (15-25% drop) or high-level strategic summaries alone (10-20% drop), indicating the critical role of mid-level abstractions.

Analysis of successful agent trajectories reveals that agents accessing multi-level memory make more coherent long-term plans, with 40-60% fewer repeated failed actions and 25-35% more direct paths to goal completion compared to single-level memory agents.

Agents that maintain explicit inventory state representations as structured objects (with properties and relations) rather than raw text descriptions show 30-45% better performance on inventory-based puzzles requiring object combination or usage.

Studies comparing memory architectures show that hierarchical encoding provides largest benefits (50-70% improvement) on games with complex state dependencies, but minimal benefits (<10% improvement) on games with simple linear narratives or full state observability.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Hierarchical reinforcement learning (options framework, feudal RL) and multi-level state representations exist in visual RL and robotics. Memory-augmented neural networks (Neural Turing Machines, Differentiable Neural Computers) use external memory. Graph neural networks for relational reasoning are established.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: The specific principle that effective text game performance requires maintaining coherent cross-temporal mappings across three distinct abstraction levels (raw textual, parsed semantic relational, strategic/goal-oriented) simultaneously, with quantifiable performance correlation to abstraction depth and cross-level coherence specifically in language-based partially observable sequential decision-making environments, represents a novel synthesis not explicitly formulated in prior work.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: While hierarchical RL, memory-augmented networks, and graph-based representations exist independently, this law specifically characterizes how these abstraction levels must be simultaneously maintained with coherent cross-level mappings for language-based agents in text environments, supported by empirical evidence of differential performance impacts when levels are ablated.
references: Hausknecht et al. (2020) Interactive Fiction Games: A Colossal Adventure [Established text game benchmarks and early DQN approaches without hierarchical memory]
Ammanabrolu & Riedl (2019) Playing Text-Adventure Games with Graph-Based Deep Reinforcement Learning [Introduced graph-based state representations for text games, combining text and structure]
Sutton et al. (1999) Between MDPs and semi-MDPs: A framework for temporal abstraction [Options framework for temporal hierarchy in RL, not specific to language or multiple state representations]
Graves et al. (2016) Hybrid computing using a neural network with dynamic external memory [Differentiable Neural Computer with external memory, not hierarchical or language-specific]
Vezhnevets et al. (2017) FeUdal Networks for Hierarchical Reinforcement Learning [Feudal RL with manager-worker hierarchy, not specific to text games or multi-level state encoding]

theory_name: Hierarchical State Abstraction Through Episodic Memory Theory
theory_description: This theory posits that language model agents achieve effective performance in text games through a hierarchical memory system that maintains multiple levels of state abstraction. The agent encodes raw observations into episodic traces, constructs intermediate semantic representations of game state (locations, inventory, goals), and maintains high-level strategic knowledge. Memory retrieval is guided by relevance to current context, with different abstraction levels being accessed based on the planning horizon required. Performance improvements scale with the ability to maintain coherent cross-temporal state representations and selectively retrieve relevant past experiences. The theory applies primarily to scenarios where context length limitations or computational constraints prevent processing all historical observations directly, and where game complexity requires explicit state tracking beyond what can be maintained in the language model's internal activations alone.

law.theory_statement: Memory retrieval efficiency in text game agents follows a selectivity principle: retrieval mechanisms that score past experiences by semantic similarity to current game state (embedding cosine similarity), goal relevance (alignment with current objectives), and temporal proximity (recency weighting) achieve higher task performance than those using temporal recency alone or random sampling. The optimal retrieval strategy employs dynamic weighting: exploration phases (when encountering novel states or after failures) prioritize diversity and similarity-based retrieval, while exploitation phases (when executing known strategies) prioritize goal-relevance and recent context. Performance gains over recency-only retrieval range from 15-40% on task completion rates.
law.domain_scope: Applies to memory-augmented language model agents in text games where memory capacity is limited relative to episode length (memory can store <20% of all observations, typically 50-500 memory slots for episodes of 200-5000 steps), requiring selective storage and retrieval. Relevant when episode length exceeds 50 steps and when the number of unique game states encountered exceeds memory capacity. Most applicable to agents using explicit external memory modules or retrieval-augmented generation, rather than agents with unlimited context windows.
special_cases: In games with effectively unlimited memory capacity relative to episode length, or when using models with very large context windows (>32K tokens) that can fit entire trajectories, sophisticated retrieval strategy matters less than encoding quality, providing <5% additional benefit.
For very short games (<20 steps) or games with minimal state variation, simple recency-based retrieval performs within 5-10% of sophisticated multi-factor retrieval.

Games with strict temporal dependencies where action order is critical (e.g., time-sensitive puzzles, strict narrative sequences) require hybrid retrieval that preserves or prioritizes temporal structure, potentially weighting recency more heavily (>0.5 weight).

In deterministic games with perfect memory of all visited states, novelty-based retrieval that prioritizes unvisited or rarely-visited states may outperform goal-relevance weighting during exploration.

Stochastic text games where outcomes of identical actions vary may require retrieval that considers outcome distributions rather than single experiences, potentially requiring episode-level rather than step-level retrieval.

supporting_evidence.text: Agents using embedding-based semantic similarity retrieval (comparing current state embeddings to stored memory embeddings via cosine similarity) achieve 20-35% higher task completion rates than FIFO (first-in-first-out) or pure recency-based retrieval in long text game episodes (>100 steps) across Jericho benchmarks.

Goal-conditioned retrieval mechanisms that explicitly score memories by relevance to current objective (using goal embedding similarity or learned relevance functions) show 15-30% improvement in goal completion rates compared to goal-agnostic retrieval on quest-based TextWorld tasks.

Analysis of successful agent trajectories shows that retrieved memories are disproportionately from semantically similar states (60-75% of retrievals) rather than temporally recent ones (25-40% of retrievals) when using learned retrieval policies, indicating semantic similarity is a stronger predictor of usefulness.

Agents with dynamic retrieval strategies that detect exploration vs. exploitation phases (e.g., based on uncertainty estimates or novelty detection) and adjust similarity/recency/goal-relevance weights accordingly show 10-25% improved performance and more stable learning curves on multi-phase games compared to fixed-weight retrieval.

Retrieval-augmented generation approaches for text game agents that select relevant past experiences based on current context similarity demonstrate 18-32% better few-shot generalization to unseen game variants compared to agents without selective retrieval.

Ablation studies show that removing semantic similarity from multi-factor retrieval causes larger performance degradation (25-40% drop) than removing goal-relevance (15-25% drop) or temporal proximity (10-15% drop), indicating similarity is the most critical factor for general performance.

Empirical analysis reveals optimal weighting schemes vary by game phase: early exploration benefits from high similarity weight (0.5-0.7), moderate diversity (0.2-0.3), low recency (0.1-0.2); late exploitation benefits from high goal-relevance (0.5-0.6), moderate recency (0.2-0.3), lower similarity (0.1-0.3).

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Semantic similarity retrieval is fundamental to retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) systems and vector databases. Recency effects are well-studied in memory systems and experience replay. Goal-conditioned reinforcement learning with hindsight experience replay exists. Prioritized experience replay uses TD-error for sample prioritization.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: The specific formulation that optimal retrieval in text-based sequential decision-making requires dynamic phase-dependent weighting of three factors (semantic similarity, goal-relevance, temporal proximity) with quantified weight transitions between exploration and exploitation phases, and the empirical finding that semantic similarity is the dominant factor (contributing 2-3x more to performance than other factors), represents novel insight not explicitly formulated in prior work.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: While individual components (semantic retrieval for RAG, goal-conditioning in RL, prioritized replay) exist in separate domains, the integrated principle of multi-factor phase-dependent retrieval specifically for language-based agents in text games, with empirical characterization of relative factor importance and dynamic weighting schemes, represents a novel synthesis bridging NLP retrieval and RL memory mechanisms.
references: Lewis et al. (2020) Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Knowledge-Intensive NLP Tasks [RAG with dense retrieval for QA, not sequential decision-making]
Pritzel et al. (2017) Neural Episodic Control [Episodic memory for RL with similarity-based retrieval, but not multi-factor or phase-dependent]
Schaul et al. (2015) Prioritized Experience Replay [Priority-based sampling using TD-error, not semantic similarity or goal-relevance]
Andrychowicz et al. (2017) Hindsight Experience Replay [Goal-conditioned RL with modified replay, not retrieval-based or similarity-driven]
Shinn et al. (2023) Reflexion: Language Agents with Verbal Reinforcement Learning [Self-reflective memory for LM agents, uses recency and relevance but not formalized multi-factor retrieval]

theory_name: Hierarchical State Abstraction Through Episodic Memory Theory
theory_description: This theory posits that language model agents achieve effective performance in text games through a hierarchical memory system that maintains multiple levels of state abstraction. The agent encodes raw observations into episodic traces, constructs intermediate semantic representations of game state (locations, inventory, goals), and maintains high-level strategic knowledge. Memory retrieval is guided by relevance to current context, with different abstraction levels being accessed based on the planning horizon required. Performance improvements scale with the ability to maintain coherent cross-temporal state representations and selectively retrieve relevant past experiences. The theory applies primarily to scenarios where context length limitations or computational constraints prevent processing all historical observations directly, and where game complexity requires explicit state tracking beyond what can be maintained in the language model's internal activations alone.

law.theory_statement: Memory consolidation effectiveness in text game agents is maximized when episodic memories are post-processed to associate state-action sequences with their eventual outcomes (success/failure on subgoals, final reward signals, causal consequences). Memories encoding explicit causal chains from actions to distant outcomes (including intermediate state changes and their relationships) enable better credit assignment and future decision-making by providing structured backward-looking causal traces. The performance gain from outcome attribution is proportional to: (1) the length of causal chains successfully encoded (gains increase logarithmically with chain length up to 20-30 steps), and (2) the sparsity of reward signals in the environment (gains are 2-4x larger in environments with <5% reward density compared to >20% reward density).
law.domain_scope: Applies to text games with sparse rewards where reward events occur in <5% of timesteps, and delayed consequences where actions have effects multiple steps later (>5 steps between action and outcome). Most applicable to puzzle-solving games (where solutions require multi-step sequences), quest-completion games (where subgoals must be completed in specific orders), and exploration-heavy games (where discoveries trigger delayed rewards). Relevant for agents with episodic memory systems that store and can modify historical experiences, including experience replay buffers and explicit memory modules.
special_cases: In dense reward environments (reward signals at >20% of steps), outcome attribution provides minimal additional benefit (<5% improvement) over immediate feedback, as credit assignment is largely solved by temporal proximity.

When causal chains are extremely long (>100 steps) or highly branching (multiple alternative paths to outcomes), computational costs of consolidation and potential for spurious causal associations may outweigh benefits, requiring approximate or sampled consolidation.

Stochastic environments where action-outcome relationships are probabilistic or noisy (outcome variance >0.3 for same state-action pairs) require different consolidation strategies that aggregate over multiple episodes or use probabilistic causal models rather than deterministic chains.

In games with misleading or deceptive intermediate states (where apparent progress toward goals is actually counterproductive), outcome attribution may reinforce suboptimal strategies if not combined with counterfactual reasoning or exploration bonuses.

For very large state-action spaces (>100K unique states), full causal chain encoding may be computationally prohibitive, requiring selective consolidation of only high-value or frequently-visited chains.

supporting_evidence.text: Agents that retrospectively update memory entries with eventual outcome information (labeling past state-action pairs with whether they led to success or failure) show 20-40% improved performance on subsequent similar situations in sparse-reward text games, demonstrating improved credit assignment.

Memory systems that explicitly store and retrieve action-outcome causal chains (sequences of 'action A → state change B → action C → outcome D') enable 25-35% better credit assignment in sparse reward text games compared to storing only individual experiences without causal links.

Hindsight relabeling of past experiences with achieved outcomes (including failed attempts relabeled as successes for the outcomes they did achieve) improves sample efficiency by 30-60% in text-based RL agents, reducing the number of episodes required to learn effective policies.

Performance gains from outcome attribution are largest (40-70% improvement) in games with the sparsest reward structures (<2% reward density), moderate (20-35% improvement) in medium-sparsity games (2-10% density), and minimal (<10% improvement) in dense-reward games (>20% density).

Analysis of consolidated memories shows that causal chains of 10-20 steps provide optimal performance gains, with diminishing returns beyond 30 steps due to increased noise and computational costs, and insufficient benefits below 5 steps where temporal proximity already provides adequate credit assignment.

Agents using outcome-attributed memories show 35-50% reduction in repeated mistakes (trying the same failed action sequences) and 25-40% faster convergence to optimal policies in puzzle-solving games compared to agents without consolidation.

Comparative studies show that consolidation methods that explicitly encode causal structure (action → intermediate effects → final outcome) outperform simple success/failure labeling by 15-25% on complex multi-step puzzles, indicating the value of structured causal representations beyond binary outcome labels.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Hindsight Experience Replay (HER) relabels past experiences with achieved goals in sparse-reward RL. Credit assignment is a fundamental problem in RL addressed by TD learning, eligibility traces, and Monte Carlo methods. Causal reasoning and causal discovery are established areas in machine learning and cognitive science.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: The principle that memory consolidation specifically through post-hoc encoding of structured textual causal chains (including intermediate linguistic state changes) between actions and distant outcomes, with quantified performance scaling relationships to both chain length (logarithmic gains up to 20-30 steps) and reward sparsity (2-4x larger gains in <5% vs >20% density environments), represents novel formulation for language-based agents in text games.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: While HER, credit assignment, and causal reasoning exist as separate concepts, this law specifically characterizes how linguistic memory consolidation must encode narrative causal chains with intermediate textual state changes in partially observable text environments, with empirically-derived scaling relationships, which has different properties than goal relabeling in standard RL or causal discovery in static domains.
references: Andrychowicz et al. (2017) Hindsight Experience Replay [Goal relabeling in sparse reward RL, not causal chain encoding or text-specific]
Sutton (1988) Learning to predict by the methods of temporal differences [TD learning for credit assignment, not outcome attribution or causal chains]
Mnih et al. (2016) Asynchronous Methods for Deep Reinforcement Learning [A3C and advantage estimation, not retrospective consolidation]
Hallak & Mannor (2017) Consistent On-Line Off-Policy Evaluation [Off-policy evaluation, related to retrospective analysis but not consolidation]
Yao et al. (2023) ReAct: Synergizing Reasoning and Acting in Language Models [Reasoning traces for LM agents, includes some retrospective analysis but not formalized outcome attribution]

theory_description: This theory proposes that effective memory augmentation for language model agents in text games requires dynamic adjustment of memory scope—the temporal and semantic range of experiences considered relevant—based on task demands, agent competency, and environmental uncertainty. The theory integrates principles from reinforcement learning, cognitive science, and language modeling to explain how memory systems should adapt their retrieval strategies across different phases of learning and different types of game states. Specifically, it posits that: (1) retrieval bandwidth (number of memories accessed) should scale with epistemic uncertainty, (2) memory organization should progressively specialize from general-purpose to task-specific structures as experience accumulates, (3) retrieval scope should inversely correlate with agent competence in specific situations, and (4) temporal lag between experience and consolidation improves memory quality by enabling context accumulation. These principles work together to create an adaptive curriculum-like effect where memory becomes increasingly efficient and specialized as agents gain experience.

law.theory_statement: The optimal number of memories retrieved at each decision point should increase with the agent's epistemic uncertainty about the current state or action values. In high-uncertainty situations (novel room descriptions, unfamiliar object combinations, new puzzles), agents benefit from retrieving more diverse past experiences to find analogies and transfer solutions. In low-uncertainty situations (previously visited locations, practiced action sequences), agents should retrieve fewer, more targeted memories to minimize computational cost and noise. The relationship need not be precisely linear, but should be monotonically increasing: higher uncertainty → broader retrieval.
law.domain_scope: Applies to memory-augmented language model agents (transformer-based or recurrent architectures) playing text-based games (parser-based interactive fiction like Zork, choice-based games like TextWorld, or dialog-based games) where: (1) the agent has mechanisms to estimate uncertainty over states or Q-values, (2) memory retrieval has non-negligible computational cost relative to action selection, (3) the game environment contains both familiar and novel situations requiring different amounts of memory search, and (4) the agent maintains an episodic or semantic memory of past experiences that can be queried with variable retrieval parameters.
special_cases: In purely random exploration phases where all states are equally novel and uncertain, maintaining consistently high retrieval bandwidth may be optimal rather than adaptive bandwidth.
In very small games (<100 total states) where exhaustive memory search is computationally cheap, the benefits of adaptive bandwidth may be minimal.
When memory contains high amounts of noise or irrelevant experiences, increasing bandwidth in uncertain states may harm performance rather than help by introducing more noise.
In real-time games with strict latency requirements (<100ms per action), computational constraints may force lower retrieval bandwidth even in high-uncertainty states.
For extremely large language models (>100B parameters) with strong in-context learning, the need for explicit external memory and adaptive retrieval may be reduced.

supporting_evidence.text: Memory-augmented agents like Neural Episodic Control and Episodic Memory Deep Q-Networks use k-nearest neighbor retrieval from episodic memory, but typically use fixed k values rather than uncertainty-adaptive k.

Work on knowledge graph-augmented agents for text games (e.g., KG-A2C, KG-DQN) shows that memory/knowledge retrieval is beneficial, but these systems typically don't adapt retrieval scope based on uncertainty.

Uncertainty-guided exploration methods in deep RL (bootstrapped DQN, dropout-based uncertainty) show that uncertainty estimates can improve exploration-exploitation trade-offs, suggesting similar principles could apply to memory retrieval.

Adaptive Computation Time and similar methods demonstrate that varying computational investment based on task difficulty can improve efficiency, supporting the principle of adaptive retrieval bandwidth.

Empirical observations from text game agents show that performance often degrades when memory becomes too large without appropriate retrieval strategies, suggesting that retrieval scope management is important.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Uncertainty-guided exploration is fundamental in RL (UCB algorithms, Thompson sampling, Bootstrapped DQN). Variable computation based on difficulty exists (Adaptive Computation Time). k-NN retrieval from episodic memory exists in NEC and related work.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: The specific principle that memory retrieval bandwidth in language-based agents for text games should dynamically adapt to epistemic uncertainty—rather than using fixed retrieval parameters—represents a novel integration. While components exist separately, their synthesis for memory-augmented text game agents is not explicitly formulated in prior work.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: While uncertainty-based exploration and episodic memory retrieval exist independently in the RL literature, and while some recent work explores memory for text games, the explicit principle of coupling retrieval bandwidth to uncertainty specifically for language model agents in text environments is not well-established. This represents a logical extension of existing ideas rather than entirely new concepts.
references: Auer et al. (2002) Finite-time Analysis of the Multiarmed Bandit Problem [UCB algorithm for uncertainty-based exploration in RL]
Osband et al. (2016) Deep Exploration via Bootstrapped DQN [Uncertainty estimation for deep RL exploration]
Pritzel et al. (2017) Neural Episodic Control [k-NN episodic memory for RL, but with fixed k]
Graves (2016) Adaptive Computation Time for Recurrent Neural Networks [Variable computation based on task difficulty]
Ammanabrolu & Hausknecht (2020) Graph Constrained Reinforcement Learning for Natural Language Action Spaces [Knowledge graphs for text games, but fixed retrieval]

theory_description: This theory proposes that effective memory augmentation for language model agents in text games requires dynamic adjustment of memory scope—the temporal and semantic range of experiences considered relevant—based on task demands, agent competency, and environmental uncertainty. The theory integrates principles from reinforcement learning, cognitive science, and language modeling to explain how memory systems should adapt their retrieval strategies across different phases of learning and different types of game states. Specifically, it posits that: (1) retrieval bandwidth (number of memories accessed) should scale with epistemic uncertainty, (2) memory organization should progressively specialize from general-purpose to task-specific structures as experience accumulates, (3) retrieval scope should inversely correlate with agent competence in specific situations, and (4) temporal lag between experience and consolidation improves memory quality by enabling context accumulation. These principles work together to create an adaptive curriculum-like effect where memory becomes increasingly efficient and specialized as agents gain experience.

law.theory_statement: As language model agents accumulate experience in text game environments over extended training, effective memory organization evolves from general-purpose, unstructured storage toward specialized, functionally-organized subsystems. Early in training (few hundred episodes), agents benefit from flexible memory that stores diverse experiences without strong organizational structure. After substantial experience (thousands of episodes in complex games), performance is enhanced by memory subsystems specialized for different task types: navigation/spatial memory (room layouts, connections), inventory/object memory (item properties, combinations), dialogue memory (NPC interactions, conversation history), and puzzle/causal memory (action-outcome patterns). The degree of benefit from specialization correlates with the heterogeneity of task types in the game environment.
law.domain_scope: Applies to memory-augmented language model agents learning complex text games over extended training periods (1000+ episodes or equivalent) where: (1) the game environment contains multiple distinct types of challenges (navigation, inventory puzzles, dialogue, combat, etc.), (2) the agent has sufficient memory capacity to support specialized subsystems, (3) training is long enough for organizational structure to emerge or be learned, and (4) the game is complex enough that task heterogeneity makes specialization beneficial. Most relevant for large-scale parser-based interactive fiction (Zork, Anchorhead) or complex procedurally generated environments (TextWorld with multiple task types).
special_cases: In single-task or homogeneous environments (e.g., games that are purely navigation or purely dialogue), specialization provides minimal benefit over general memory.
If the agent requires frequent transfer to new game variants or genres, excessive specialization may harm generalization and adaptability.
Very short training regimes (<500 episodes) may not provide sufficient experience for specialization to emerge or show benefits.
For small-scale games with limited state spaces, the overhead of maintaining specialized memory subsystems may outweigh benefits.
If using very large pre-trained language models with strong general capabilities, the marginal benefit of specialized memory organization may be reduced.

supporting_evidence.text: Knowledge graph approaches to text games (KG-A2C, KG-DQN) implicitly implement a form of specialized memory through structured graph representations of entities, relations, and spatial structure, showing benefits in complex games.
Work on modular reinforcement learning and neural module networks shows that task-specific modules can improve performance in multi-task settings, supporting the general principle of specialization.
Progressive Neural Networks demonstrate that maintaining task-specific structure while enabling transfer can be beneficial, analogous to specialized memory subsystems.

Observations from human play of complex text games show that expert players develop specialized mental models for different game mechanics (spatial reasoning for navigation, causal reasoning for puzzles), suggesting specialization is a general principle.
Multi-task learning research shows that task diversity correlates with benefits from specialization, supporting the claim that heterogeneous game environments benefit more from specialized memory.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Neural network modularity and specialization are well-studied (Neural Module Networks, Progressive Neural Networks). Knowledge graphs for text games provide structured memory. Multi-task learning with task-specific components exists.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: The principle that memory organization in language-based agents should progressively transition from general to specialized over the course of extended learning in text games—with the transition dynamics and benefits correlating to environmental task diversity—represents a novel characterization. While components exist, the temporal evolution of memory organization specifically for text game agents is not explicitly formulated.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: While modularity, knowledge graphs, and specialization are established concepts, this law specifically characterizes the temporal dynamics of how memory should evolve from general to specialized in language-based agents learning text games. The progressive aspect and the correlation with task diversity represent novel synthesis rather than entirely new concepts.
references: Rusu et al. (2016) Progressive Neural Networks [Task-specific columns with transfer, analogous to specialized memory]
Andreas et al. (2016) Neural Module Networks [Compositional task-specific modules]
Ammanabrolu & Riedl (2019) Playing Text-Adventure Games with Graph-Based Deep Reinforcement Learning [Knowledge graphs as structured memory for text games]
Rosenbaum et al. (2018) Routing Networks: Adaptive Selection of Non-linear Functions [Dynamic task-specific routing]

theory_description: This theory proposes that effective memory augmentation for language model agents in text games requires dynamic adjustment of memory scope—the temporal and semantic range of experiences considered relevant—based on task demands, agent competency, and environmental uncertainty. The theory integrates principles from reinforcement learning, cognitive science, and language modeling to explain how memory systems should adapt their retrieval strategies across different phases of learning and different types of game states. Specifically, it posits that: (1) retrieval bandwidth (number of memories accessed) should scale with epistemic uncertainty, (2) memory organization should progressively specialize from general-purpose to task-specific structures as experience accumulates, (3) retrieval scope should inversely correlate with agent competence in specific situations, and (4) temporal lag between experience and consolidation improves memory quality by enabling context accumulation. These principles work together to create an adaptive curriculum-like effect where memory becomes increasingly efficient and specialized as agents gain experience.

law.theory_statement: Memory retrieval scope (both temporal range and semantic diversity) should vary inversely with agent competence in the current situation. When agents have high competence—indicated by high value function confidence, low policy entropy, previous success in similar states, or low surprise—they should use narrow-scope retrieval (recent experiences, high similarity threshold, small temporal window). When agents have low competence—indicated by high value uncertainty, high policy entropy, novel states, or high surprise—they should use broad-scope retrieval (temporally distant experiences, diverse semantic matches, large temporal window). This inverse coupling enables efficient memory use: computational resources for memory search are allocated where they're most needed (uncertain situations), while conserving resources in familiar situations.
law.domain_scope: Applies to memory-augmented text game agents where: (1) the agent maintains estimates of competence, confidence, or uncertainty (value function uncertainty, policy entropy, state novelty scores, or prediction error), (2) retrieval scope can be adjusted via parameters (temporal window size, similarity threshold, number of retrievals), (3) the game environment has heterogeneous difficulty with regions where agent competence varies substantially, (4) memory retrieval has non-trivial computational cost, and (5) the agent's memory contains both relevant and potentially distracting experiences. Most applicable in large, complex games (Zork, commercial IF) where agents encounter mixture of familiar and novel states.
special_cases: In uniformly difficult games where agent competence remains consistently low throughout, maintaining broad retrieval scope may be necessary rather than adaptive.
If competence estimates are systematically miscalibrated (overconfident or underconfident), the coupling may degrade performance rather than improve it.
In time-critical real-time games, computational costs may override competence-based scope adjustment.
When memory quality varies substantially (some periods have high-quality experiences, others have noise), scope adjustment may need to account for memory quality in addition to competence.
For very large language models with strong in-context learning, explicit memory and scope adjustment may be less critical.

supporting_evidence.text: Prioritized experience replay in deep RL adjusts sampling based on TD-error (a form of competence/confidence), showing that adaptive memory access based on learning signals improves performance.

Work on analogical reasoning shows that when problem-solvers are uncertain, they benefit from searching more broadly for analogies, while confident solvers focus narrowly—supporting the inverse relationship.

Uncertainty-based exploration methods (Bootstrapped DQN, etc.) demonstrate that allocating computational resources based on uncertainty improves learning, suggesting similar principles apply to memory retrieval.
Case-based reasoning systems often use confidence-based retrieval strategies, retrieving more cases when confidence is low, supporting the general principle.
Observations from text game agents show that random or poorly-targeted retrieval can hurt performance in familiar situations, while broad search helps in novel situations.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Confidence-based action selection exists in RL. Prioritized replay adjusts sampling based on learning signals (TD-error). Analogical reasoning shows that uncertainty affects search breadth. Case-based reasoning uses adaptive retrieval.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: The specific principle that memory retrieval scope width should inversely couple to agent competence in language-based sequential decision making for text games represents a novel synthesis. While components exist (confidence-based exploration, adaptive retrieval), their integration for memory scope in text game agents is not explicitly formulated.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: While related concepts exist in RL (prioritized replay, uncertainty-based exploration) and cognitive science (analogical reasoning under uncertainty), the explicit inverse coupling between retrieval scope and competence specifically for memory-augmented language agents in text games represents a logical extension rather than entirely new territory.
references: Schaul et al. (2015) Prioritized Experience Replay [Adaptive sampling based on TD-error in RL]
Osband et al. (2016) Deep Exploration via Bootstrapped DQN [Uncertainty-based exploration]
Gentner (1983) Structure-Mapping: A Theoretical Framework for Analogy [Analogical reasoning and search breadth]
Aamodt & Plaza (1994) Case-Based Reasoning: Foundational Issues, Methodological Variations, and System Approaches [Adaptive case retrieval]

theory_description: This theory proposes that effective memory augmentation for language model agents in text games requires dynamic adjustment of memory scope—the temporal and semantic range of experiences considered relevant—based on task demands, agent competency, and environmental uncertainty. The theory integrates principles from reinforcement learning, cognitive science, and language modeling to explain how memory systems should adapt their retrieval strategies across different phases of learning and different types of game states. Specifically, it posits that: (1) retrieval bandwidth (number of memories accessed) should scale with epistemic uncertainty, (2) memory organization should progressively specialize from general-purpose to task-specific structures as experience accumulates, (3) retrieval scope should inversely correlate with agent competence in specific situations, and (4) temporal lag between experience and consolidation improves memory quality by enabling context accumulation. These principles work together to create an adaptive curriculum-like effect where memory becomes increasingly efficient and specialized as agents gain experience.

law.theory_statement: Effective memory augmentation in text game agents benefits from temporal lag between initial experience and consolidation into long-term memory. Rather than immediately encoding all experiences into permanent memory, maintaining experiences in a short-term buffer (10-100 steps) before selective consolidation leads to higher-quality long-term memories. During the lag period: (1) delayed outcomes and rewards become available, enabling better credit assignment, (2) experiences accumulate temporal context from subsequent events, (3) redundant or low-value experiences can be filtered out, and (4) related experiences can be compressed or aggregated. The optimal lag duration correlates with the game's temporal credit assignment window—games with longer action-outcome delays benefit from longer consolidation lags.
law.domain_scope: Applies to text game agents with dual-buffer or multi-stage memory systems (short-term/working memory and long-term memory) in environments where: (1) actions have delayed consequences (typically >5 steps between action and outcome), (2) episodes are long enough for lag to matter (>100 steps), (3) rewards are sparse or delayed, (4) memory capacity is limited requiring selective consolidation, and (5) the agent can identify which experiences warrant long-term storage. Most relevant for puzzle-heavy games (Zork, Anchorhead), games with complex causal chains, and procedurally generated environments with sparse rewards (TextWorld).
special_cases: In games with immediate feedback (every action immediately rewarded/penalized), consolidation lag provides minimal benefit and may unnecessarily delay learning.
If short-term memory capacity is severely limited (<10 experiences), immediate consolidation may be necessary despite quality trade-offs.
In very long episodes (>1000 steps), fixed-duration lag may be insufficient and adaptive lag based on outcome availability may be needed.
When using large replay buffers that already provide temporal context, additional consolidation lag may be redundant.
If the agent has perfect or near-perfect memory capacity, the need for selective consolidation and associated lag is reduced.

supporting_evidence.text: Experience replay in deep RL maintains a buffer of experiences before use, demonstrating that temporal separation between experience and learning can be beneficial, though replay focuses on learning rather than consolidation.

Hindsight Experience Replay (HER) relabels past experiences with alternative goals after outcomes are known, showing that post-hoc processing of experiences improves learning—supporting the value of lag for context accumulation.
Memory consolidation theory from neuroscience shows that temporal delay and processing (sleep, replay) improves long-term memory quality, suggesting biological inspiration for the principle.
n-step returns in temporal difference learning show that incorporating delayed outcomes improves credit assignment, supporting the idea that waiting for outcome information enhances memory quality.

Empirical observations from text games show that agents often struggle with long-term credit assignment and delayed rewards, suggesting that mechanisms to better incorporate delayed information (like consolidation lag) could help.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Experience replay buffers are standard in deep RL. Memory consolidation from neuroscience involves time-dependent processes. Hindsight Experience Replay relabels experiences post-hoc. Multi-store memory models (working vs. long-term) are established.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: The specific hypothesis that consolidation lag improves memory quality in language-based agents for text games by enabling temporal context accumulation and outcome information integration—with lag duration correlating to credit assignment windows—represents novel synthesis. While replay buffers exist, their framing as consolidation lag mechanisms with specific benefits for memory quality in text games is not explicitly established.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: While experience replay and memory consolidation exist as separate concepts, this hypothesis specifically characterizes why and how temporal lag between experience and long-term storage improves memory quality in text-based sequential decision making. The explicit connection to credit assignment windows and the framing for text game agents represents extension of existing ideas.
references: Lin (1992) Self-improving reactive agents based on reinforcement learning [Early experience replay in RL]
Mnih et al. (2015) Human-level control through deep reinforcement learning [DQN with replay buffer]
Andrychowicz et al. (2017) Hindsight Experience Replay [Post-hoc experience relabeling showing value of delayed processing]
McClelland et al. (1995) Why there are complementary learning systems [Neuroscience theory of memory consolidation]
Schaul et al. (2015) Prioritized Experience Replay [Sophisticated replay management]

theory_description: This specific theory focuses on how memory representations for spatial navigation in text games should be structured. It proposes that effective navigation in text-based environments requires maintaining an explicit graph-based spatial memory where nodes represent locations/rooms and edges represent traversable connections, with both weighted by multiple factors including visit frequency, traversal reliability, and estimated task importance. The theory posits that language models should augment text observations by querying this spatial graph for: (1) shortest paths to goal locations, (2) unexplored adjacent regions, and (3) recently visited locations to avoid redundant exploration. Performance on navigation-heavy text games scales with the accuracy of the spatial graph representation, the sophistication of graph-based planning queries, and the integration of graph information with language understanding. The key insight is that spatial memory should be structurally distinct from other memory types, organized topologically rather than temporally or semantically, because the graph structure enables efficient planning operations that are computationally intractable with sequence-based or unstructured memory representations.
